research_study_list.title: Training Data Subset Selection for Regression with Controlled Generalization Error
research_study_list.abstract:
Data subset selection from a large number of training instances has been a
successful approach toward efficient and cost-effective machine learning.
However, models trained on a smaller subset may show poor generalization
ability. In this paper, our goal is to design an algorithm for selecting a
subset of the training data, so that the model can be trained quickly, without
significantly sacrificing on accuracy. More specifically, we focus on data
subset selection for L2 regularized regression problems and provide a novel
problem formulation which seeks to minimize the training loss with respect to
both the trainable parameters and the subset of training data, subject to error
bounds on the validation set. We tackle this problem using several technical
innovations. First, we represent this problem with simplified constraints using
the dual of the original training problem and show that the objective of this
new representation is a monotone and alpha-submodular function, for a wide
variety of modeling choices. Such properties lead us to develop SELCON, an
efficient majorization-minimization algorithm for data subset selection, that
admits an approximation guarantee even when the training provides an imperfect
estimate of the trained model. Finally, our experiments on several datasets
show that SELCON trades off accuracy and efficiency more effectively than the
current state-of-the-art.
research_study_list.full_text: Training Data Subset Selection for Regression with Controlled Generalization Error Durga Sivasubramanian1, Rishabh Iyer2, Ganesh Ramakrishnan1, and Abir De1 1IIT Bombay, {durgas, ganesh, abir }@cse.iitb.ac.in 2UT Dallas, rishabh.iyer@utdallas.edu Abstract Data subset selection from a large number of training instances has been a successful approach toward efÔ¨Ācient and cost-effective machine learning. However, models trained on a smaller subset may show poor generalization ability. In this paper, our goal is to design an algorithm for selecting a subset of the training data, so that the model can be trained quickly, without signiÔ¨Ācantly sacriÔ¨Ācing on accuracy. More speciÔ¨Ācally, we focus on data subset selection for L2 regularized regression problems and provide a novel problem formulation which seeks to minimize the training loss with respect to both the trainable parameters and the subset of training data, subject to error bounds on the validation set. We tackle this problem using several technical innovations. First, we represent this problem with simpliÔ¨Āed constraints using the dual of the original training problem and show that the objective of this new representation is a monotone and őĪ-submodular function, for a wide variety of modeling choices. Such properties lead us to develop SELCON, an efÔ¨Ācient majorization-minimization algorithm for data subset selection, that admits an approximation guarantee even when the training provides an imperfect estimate of the trained model. Finally, our experiments on several datasets show that SELCON trades off accuracy and efÔ¨Āciency more effectively than the current state-of-the-art. 1 Introduction Data-driven estimation of the underlying statistical model is the central challenge in any supervised machine learning (ML) problem. Thanks to the law of large numbers [ 7], such a training procedure often demands a huge number of training examples to ensure statistical reliability of the learned model. Therefore, the success of several machine learning models can be attributed to the availability of a massive amount of data and thus to the high performance computing infrastructures, e.g., GPUs, multicore processors, high storage disks, etc., which are required to store and process such data. These computational resources involve large expenses, additional energy utilization and maintenance costs. Mitigation of such overheads without sacriÔ¨Ācing the accuracy of the predictive model is a challenging task, which often entails a careful selection of a smaller number of training instances, so that the training algorithm can be run in an environment with limited resources [32, 33, 5, 24, 26, 41, 31, 2, 27]. However, current data selection techniques do not explicitly account for the generalization error which may be exacerbated in the presence of a small sized training dataset. As a consequence, they can suffer from high generalization error, especially for large datasets. 1.1 Present work In response to the above limitations, our goal is to select a subset from training data in such a way that the model can be quickly trained in an environment with limited resources, while at the same time, provide good predictive power. More speciÔ¨Ācally, we make the following contributions. Novel formulation of data selection. In this work, we focus on the regression problem and introduce a novel problem formulation (Section 2) - which encodes the task of data selection for regression, while ensuring that the error on validation set remains below an acceptable level. Such an explicit use of the validation set during training improves the generalization ability of the inferred model, as indicated in [37, 26]. 1 arXiv:2106.12491v1  [cs.LG]  23 Jun 2021More speciÔ¨Ācally, given a model class and a Ô¨Āxed validation set, we seek to minimize an L2 regularized constrained squared error loss with respect to both the parameter vector and the subset of training data, subject to a set of error bounds on different portions of the validation set. The use of such error bounds as optimization constraints enhances the generalization ability of the inferred model in the face of small training data. Moreover, the presence of multiple error constraints in our setup can be useful in several data selection problems; e.g., learning with heterogeneous data where each constraint limits the error for each cluster of data [38]; fair regression with bounded group loss, where each constraint limits the error on the protected group(s), etc. [1]. In general, our data selection problem is NP-hard due to the presence of both the training set and the model parameters as optimization variables. However, it permits us to reformulate it into a new optimization task with simpliÔ¨Āed constraints, by making use of the Lagrangian dual of the original training problem. This new optimization problem can be seen as an instance of cardinality-constrained set function minimization problem, where the objective corresponds to the optimal training loss as a function of the candidate data subset. Characterizing the loss function of data selection. Having represented the optimal training loss as a set function, we show that this function is monotone, őĪ-submodular [13, 30, 16] and enjoys a bounded generalized curvature [23, 46] for a wide variety of models including a class of nonlinear functions (Section 3). These technical results can be useful in other related data selection problems and therefore, are of independent interest. Approximation algorithm for data selection. Finally, to solve our data selection problem, we design SELCON, a new majorization-minorization algorithm (Algorithm 1, Section 4) building upon the semi-differentials proposed by [22, 21], which minimizes the set function characterized above. SELCON enjoys an approximation guarantee even when the training algorithm provides an imperfect estimate. While doing so, we obtain a new family of modular upper bounds of an őĪ-submodular function, which extends the bounds proposed in [22] and therefore, can be of independent technical interests. Moreover, SELCON can minimize any monotone, őĪ-submodular function, going beyond the particular instance in this work, which makes it useful from a broader perspective. We evaluate1 our framework on several real-world datasets, and demonstrate that SELCON trades off the accuracy and efÔ¨Āciency more effectively than several baselines and state-of-the-art. We also demonstrate that the use of constrained validation set error maintains the generalization ability of the inferred model in the presence of small training data. Finally, we test our framework on the application of fair regression with bounded group loss, which shows that SELCON offers fair prediction along with an effective trade-off between accuracy and efÔ¨Āciency. 1.2 Related work Algorithms for data selection predominantly follow two approaches. The Ô¨Ārst approach [41, 43, 31, 2] selects diverse training examples by maximizing submodular proxy functions, e.g., facility location, etc., and then use them to train the underlying model. The second approach selects coresets ‚Äď weighted subsets of training examples ‚Äď alongside training the model over them [32, 33, 25, 6, 5, 24]. The choice of a coreset depends strongly on the model as well as on the training loss. Therefore, coreset selection algorithms vary widely across different ML settings, e.g., SVM [8], Bayesian inference [6], k-means clustering [14], regression [5], deep learning [33, 25], etc.However, they do not explicitly control the validation set error, which often constrains their predictive power. Our work is related to robust and efÔ¨Ācient learning methods [37, 47, 26], that utilize the validation set to improve the training performance via a bi-level optimization. However, these approaches do not explicitly control the validation set error the way we do. Our work is also related to subset selection problems in the context of human-assisted machine learning [10, 11], that aim to select a training subset to outsource to humans, rather than facilitating efÔ¨Ācient learning. Moreover, unlike us, these setups do not consider any validation constraint. Our work is also connected with batch active learning methods [42, 15, 29, 39], that aim to select examples from training data in order to minimize the labeling cost. In contrast, our setup has access to all the labels and it aims to select data to improve efÔ¨Āciency. In recent years, there is a Ô¨āurry of works on maximizing non-submodular functions [ 19, 9, 4, 28, 13, 18, 17]. However, there is a paucity of work on minimizingőĪ-submodular functions. Very recently, Halabiet al. [12] aim to minimize the difference between two monotone őĪ-submodular and ő≤-submodular functions. However, they do not consider a cardinality constraint, which makes their approach less relevant to our setting. 1Our code and data is available at https://github.com/abir-de/SELCON 22 Problem formulation In the following, we Ô¨Ārst setup the notation and contextualize our problem. Thereafter, we formally present our data selection problem which involves simultaneous selection of a subset Sof the training dataset Dand training of a regression model y ‚Čąhw(x), subject to validation error constraints. We obtain an alternative representation of this problem, using the Lagrangian dual of the parameter estimation task. Finally we formally show that our data selection problem is NP-Hard. 2.1 Notation Let {xi,yi}i‚ąąDbe the set of training samples and {xj,yj}j‚ąąVthe set of validation samples. Here, x‚ÄĘ‚ąąRd are the features and y‚ÄĘ‚ąąR are the corresponding response (output) variables. We also have a partition of Qsubsets over the validation set, i.e., V= V1 ‚ą™V2 ‚ą™... ‚ą™VQ. Unless otherwise stated, ‚ą•¬∑‚ą•denotes the L2 norm, i.e., ‚ą•x‚ą•= ‚ąö x‚ä§x. 2.2 Our broad objective We are provided a modeling frameworkhw : Rd ‚ÜíR which can approximate the relationship between xand y, i.e., y‚Čąhw(x), where wis a trainable parameter vector. Given the aforementioned setup, one can learn wusing standard least square estimation. In principle, one might be tempted to estimate wusing the entire set of training examples D, which would possibly give a statistically sound estimate of w. However, if the size of Dis large, such exhaustive training may be inefÔ¨Ācient in a typical computing environment. To tackle this problem, our goal is to determine a smaller subset of training samples S‚äāD such that it allows for efÔ¨Ācient training of the model without signiÔ¨Ācant drop in accuracy. 2.3 Problem setting for data selection Given the full training set {xi,yi}i‚ąąDand the validation set {xj,yj}j‚ąąValong with its partitions V= ‚ą™q‚ąą[Q]Vq and the model class hw, we consider minimization of the L2 regularized training loss, jointly with respect to parameters wand the candidate subset S, subject to a set of constraints that bound the mean squared errors (MSE) on the Q partitioning subsets of the validation set, i.e., minimize S‚äāD,w ‚ąĎ i‚ąąS [őĽ‚ą•w‚ą•2 + (yi ‚ąíhw(xi))2], subject to, ‚ąĎ j‚ąąVq (yj ‚ąíhw(xj))2 |Vq| ‚Č§őī, ‚ąÄq‚ąą[Q], |S|= k. (1) Here, őĽis the coefÔ¨Ācient of the regularizer; the cardinality constraint limits the number of training samples to be chosen; and the validation error constraints ensure that the predictor‚Äôs loss remains below some acceptable levelőīfor the subsets {Vq}of the validation set2. Discussion on multiple validation error bounds. Note that the absence of validation error constraints in the basic problem setting may result in efÔ¨Ācient training, but might not generalize well owing to small size of the training data. The validation error constraints in (1) ameliorate this problem, by attenuating the generalization error which might have exacerbated in the face of a small sized training data. For example, in the case of fair regression with bounded group loss, the validation set can be partitioned in a way that each subset Vq corresponds to the sub-population for a protected group, so that the individual MSE for each protected group remains small. Our setup can also be useful in learning from heterogeneous data, wherein the heterogeneity could have arisen owing to multiple sources of data, time-shifts in the distribution,etc.. (13) Subset selection for linear regression problems has been widely studied in literature [15, 10]. Except for [10], these approaches optimize measures associated with the covariance matrix, rather than explicitly minimizing the training loss subject to the validation set error bound. While [ 10] also optimizes the training loss, it applies to a completely different application of learning under human assistance, which is why it does not take into account the validation set error bound. 3.3 Generalized curvature Next, we provide a uniÔ¨Āed bound on the generalized curvature (c.f., DeÔ¨Ānition 4) for both linear and nonlinear modeling choices of hw(x), as formalized in the following proposition (proven in Appendix B.4). Proposition 8 Given the assumptions stated in Theorem 6, the generalized curvature kf(S) for any set SsatisÔ¨Āes őļf(S) ‚Č§ňÜőļf = 1‚ąí ‚Ąď‚ąó (CQ+ 1)y2max . 4 The S ELCON algorithm In this section, we design SELCON, an iterative approximation algorithm to minimize f(S), by leveraging the semi- differential based approach proposed by Iyeret al. [22]. However, they only consider submodular optimization problems having access to an exact measurement of the objective. In contrast, SELCON works for őĪ-submodular functions and enjoys an approximation guarantee even when it can only access an imperfect estimate of the learned parameters. 4.1 Outline of S ELCON At the very outset, SELCON is an iterative Majorization-Minimization algorithm for minimizing a monotone őĪ- submodular function. We Ô¨Ārst develop a modular upper bound of f(S). Then, at each iteration, we minimize this upper bound and reÔ¨Āne the estimate of the candidate set S. Modular upper bound of f(S). Given an őĪ-submodular function f and a Ô¨Āxed set ňÜS, we can obtain the modular upper bound of f(S), as follows (see details in Appendix C.1). Lemma 9 Given a Ô¨Āxed set ňÜSand an őĪ-submodular function f(S), let the modular function mf ňÜS[S] be deÔ¨Āned as follows: mf ňÜS[S] =f( ňÜS) ‚ąí ‚ąĎ i‚ąąňÜS őĪf(i|ňÜS\{i}) + ‚ąĎ i‚ąąňÜS‚ą©S őĪf(i|ňÜS\{i}) + ‚ąĎ i‚ąąS\ňÜS f(i|‚ąÖ) őĪ . Results with perfect parameter estimates. In the following, we present our Ô¨Ārst result on the approximation guarantee (proof in Appendix C.2). Theorem 10 If the training algorithm in Algorithm 1 (lines 3, 6, 8) provides perfect estimates of the model parameters, it obtains a set ňÜSwhich satisÔ¨Āes: f( ňÜS) ‚Č§ k ňÜőĪf(1 + (k‚ąí1)(1 ‚ąíňÜőļf)ňÜőĪf)f(S‚ąó) (15) where ňÜőĪf and ňÜőļf are as stated in Theorem 6 and Proposition 8 respectively. Results with imperfect parameter estimates. Data-driven training algorithms may not provide the optimal value of model parameters, even if the underlying loss function is convex. Therefore, in practice, SELCON can only access an imperfect estimate of ňÜw,ňÜ¬Ķin lines 3, 6 and 8. Submodular and weakly submodular optimization in the presence of imperfect estimates has been widely studied in literature [36, 12, 18, 17, 19, 40]. However, to the best of our knowledge, they do not tackle the problem of cardinality-constrained minimization of an őĪ-submodular function. In this context, a notable contribution of our work is that, SELCON also enjoys a relaxed approximation guarantee in these cases, which renders it practically useful. We formally state the result as follows (proven in Appendix C.3). A trite calculation shows that, for the regime of őĽdeÔ¨Āned in Theorem 6 and a small value of ŌĶ, the approximation ratio of SELCON is O(y4 max/y4 min). While such a ratio may appear to be conservative, there are several applications such as house price prediction or stock prediction, whereymax/ymin may not be too high. Apart from that, one can always pre-process the dataset by adding an offset to yand augmenting a constant in the feature x, to control this ratio, as illustrated in Appendix E. Moreover, since our approximation ratio holds for any monotone őĪ-submodular function with bounded curvature, it can be of independent technical interest. 5 Experiments In this section, we present experimental results and analysis on several real-world datasets to evaluate the performance of SELCON against several competitive baselines. Thereafter, we show that our framework is also practically useful in a fair regression setup, where the validation loss bounds are used to ensure that the error for each protected group is below an acceptable level of threshold. Appendix E contains additional experiments. 5.1 Experimental setup Datasets. We experiment with Ô¨Āve real world datasets, viz., Cadata (16718 instances), Law (20800 instances), NYSE- High (701348 instances), NYSE-Close (701348 instances), and Community-and-crime (1994 instances), all brieÔ¨āy described in Appendix D. Baselines. We compare SELCON against seven baselines. (1) Full-selection: It uses full data for training without any validation error constraint. (2) Full-with-constraints: It uses full data for training, subject to the same validation error constraints used in SELCON. (3) Random-selection: It samples a training subset uniformly at random, but it does not employ any constraint on validation set. (4) Random-with-constraints: It is the same as Random-selection, except that it uses the constraints on validation set errors. (5) CRAIG [33]: This is a coreset based data selection method, that however, does not use any constraint on the validation set. (6) GLISTER [26]: This is a data selection method that uses validation set to Ô¨Āne tune the trained model, which however, does not pose any explicit constraint on the validation set error. (7) SELCON-without-constraint: Here, we solve the optimization problem (2), without the validation error constraints. Implementation details. In Algorithm 1, if we set the number of epochs for Train( )in line 6 to T, this training routine runs for N = LT epochs, where Lis the number of iterations of the for-loop (lines 5‚Äď14). To make a fair comparison, we used the same number of epochs N and the same batch size bacross all baselines and SELCON for training the underlying model. SpeciÔ¨Ācally, we set N = 2000for Cadata and Law , N = 5000for the NYSE datasets; and, b= min{|S|,1000}across all datasets. Additionally, SELCON involves two more sets of small scale optimization problems (lines 3 and 8 respectively), where we set the number of epochs as 3. Moreover for the optimization of f(S\{i}) in line 8, we use the same batch size b = min{|S|,1000}as stated earlier. In each experiment, we used (random) 89% training, 1% validation and 10% test folds. We employed pytorch with the adam optimizer for all experiments. Further details about the implementation are provided in Appendix D. 5.2 Predictive performance and efÔ¨Āciency We evaluate the performance of each data selection method in terms of the mean squared error (MSE) E[(y‚ąíňÜy)2] on the test set. We also compute the computational efÔ¨Āciency of a method in terms of the speed-up it achieves with respect to Full-selection, i.e., RunTimeFull-selection/RunTimemethod, where RunTime‚ÄĘis time taken by the corresponding method to complete both the subset selection and model training. To that end, we apply our approach to the Law and Community-and-crime datasets [ 1] and enforce fairness violation measured in terms of E[|(yi ‚ąíňÜyi)2 ‚ąí (yj ‚ąíňÜyj)2| ‚Źź‚Źźi ‚ąąVq,j ‚ąą V\Vq] (Q = 8 in Law, Q = 4 in Community-and-crime) in the dataset, we partition the validation set Vinto the subsets V1,..,V Q, so that each subset Vq consists of individuals with the race rq, i.e., Vq = {(xj,yj) |Race of individual j = rq}. Results. In Figure 4, we plot the performance of SELCON in terms of the mean fairness violation, as measured by E[|(yi ‚ąíňÜyi)2 ‚ąí(yj ‚ąíňÜyj)2| ‚Źź‚Źźi‚ąąVq,j ‚ąąV\Vq] for various values of őī. We compare SELCON‚Äôs performance against 5We omitted the results for CRAIG in nonlinear regression because the data selection component of CRAIG needs to be run for several epochs for non-convex losses [33], and hence, it did not scale for the large datasets. 11Full-with-constraints and Random-with-constraints, the only other methods that can enforce fairness by means of error constraints on the validation set. Evidently,SELCON guarantees fairness more effectively than Random-with-constraints. Moreover, for low values of őī, the performance of SELCON is close to Full-with-constraints. 6 Conclusion We presented a novel data subset selection formulation that aims to select a subset Sby controlling the generalization errors. SpeciÔ¨Ācally, we considered L2 regularized regression over candidate training setS, subject to the error bounds on different partitions of the validation set. Such error bounds reduce the generalization error that could otherwise increase owing to training on a small sized data. Thereafter, we reformulated our data selection task as a new optimization problem and showed its equivalence to minimization of a monotone and őĪ-submodular function. Finally, we designed a majorization-minimization based approximation algorithm SELCON to solve this problem in the face of imperfect training. Our experiments show that SELCON can more effectively trade off between accuracy and efÔ¨Āciency than several baselines. Our work opens several areas for future work;e.g., it can be extended to data selection for classiÔ¨Ācation as well as data removal for outlier detection. C.4 Convergence property We begin this section by showing that SELCON is guaranteed to reduce the objective value at every iteration as long as we obtain perfect solutions from the training algorithm (lines 3, 6, 8 in Algorithm 1). Lemma 14 SELCON (Algorithm 1) is guaranteed to reduce the objective value of f at every iteration as long as we obtain perfect solutions from the training sub-routine. Proof SELCON essentially uses modular upper bounds mf of f at every iteration. Denote Sl as the set obtained in the lth iteration and let Sl+1 be the one from the l+ 1th iteration. Then the following chain of inequalities hold: f(Sl+1) ‚Č§mf(Sl+1) ‚Č§mf(Sl) =f(Sl) (83) The Ô¨Ārst inequality holds because mf is a modular upper bound, the second inequality holds becauseSl+1 is the solution of minimizing mf (and hence mf(Sl=1) is lower in value compared to mf(Sl)). The last equality holds because mf is a modular upper bound which is tight at Sl and hence mf(Sl) =f(Sl). This shows that f(Sl+1) ‚Č§f(Sl). We end this section by pointing out that this chain of inequalities does not hold if we get inexact or approximation solutions to the training sub-routine. In practice, we observe that the objective value of f still reduces even though we obtain only inexact solutions since the inexact solutions are often close to the true solutions of the training step. 25D Additional details about experimental setup D.1 Dataset details ‚ÄĘ Cadata: California housing dataset is obtained from the LIBSVM package 6. This spatial dataset contains 20,640 observations on housing prices with 9 economic covariates. As described in [35], here xare information about households in a block, say median age, median income, total rooms/population, bedrooms/population, population/households,households etc. and yis median price in median housing prices by all California census blocks. It has dimension(x) =8. ‚ÄĘ Law: This refers to the dataset on Law School Admissions Council‚Äôs National Longitudinal Bar Passage Study [44]. Here xis information about a law student, including information on gender, race, family income, age, etc. and yindicates GPA normalised to [0,1] . We use race as a protected attribute for the fairness experiments. It has dimension(x) =10. ‚ÄĘ NYSE-High: This dataset is obtained from the New York stock exchange (NYSE)7 dataset as follows. Given the set {si}with si corresponding to the the highest stock price of the ith day, we deÔ¨Āne sk+1 = ‚ąĎ i‚ąą[100] wisk+1‚ąíi. Here yk = sk+1 and xk = [sk,sk‚ąí1,...,s k‚ąí99]. This dataset has dimension(x) = 100. ‚ÄĘ NYSE-close: This dataset is obtained from the New York stock exchange (NYSE)8 dataset as follows. Given the set {si}with si corresponding to the the closing stock price of the ith day, we deÔ¨Āne sk+1 = ‚ąĎ i‚ąą[100] wisk+1‚ąíi. Here yk = sk+1 and xk = [sk,sk‚ąí1,...,s k‚ąí99]. This dataset has dimension(x) = 100. D.2 Implementation details Our models. We use two models‚ÄĒ a simple linear regression model and a two layer neural network that consists of a linear layer of 5 hidden nodes and a ReLU activation unit. In all our experiments, we use a learning rate of 0.01. We choose the value of őīas the 30% of the mean validation error obtained using Full-selection. Implementation of CRAIG. CRAIG [33] requires computing a D√óD matrix with similarity measure for each pair of points in the training set. For the larger datasets, i.e., NYSE-close and NYSE-high, such a computation requires a large amount of memory. Hence, we use a stochastic version where we randomly select Rpoints and build R√óR matrix and select kR D each time and repeat the process D R times. We use R= 50000. Note that, for other datasets, since |D|<50000 the stochastic version is same as the original version. CRAIG requires us to select the subset only once, since features will not change even as the training proceeds. However, since CRAIG is an adaptive method, for the non-linear setting, we need to run CRAIG every epoch. Despite using the stochastic version, we found CRAIG to be very slow in the non-linear setting and therefore we don‚Äôt report it. Implementation of GLISTER. GLISTER [26], an another adaptive subset selection method where we select a new subset every 35th epoch to help make a fair comparison against SELCON. We update the model parameters after every selection step. Machine conÔ¨Āguration. We performed our experiments on a computer system with Ubuntu 16.04.6 LTS, an i-7 with 6 cores CPU and a total RAM of 125 GBs. The system had a single GeForce GTX 1080 GPU which was employed in our experiments. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces a new training data subset selection formulation for L2-regularized regression that explicitly constrains validation error (optionally across multiple validation partitions) to control generalization when training on small subsets. Reformulates the problem via the Lagrangian dual to a cardinality-constrained set-function minimization and proves the resulting objective is monotone and őĪ-submodular (with bounded generalized curvature) for broad model classes, including certain nonlinear models. Proposes SELCON, a majorization‚Äďminimization algorithm using novel modular upper bounds for őĪ-submodular functions, with approximation guarantees even under imperfect training. Demonstrates superior accuracy‚Äďefficiency trade-offs over strong baselines and applies the framework to fair regression with bounded group loss.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Problem: Jointly select S‚äāD (|S|=k) and parameters w to minimize ‚ąĎ_{i‚ąąS}[őĽ||w||^2+(y_i‚ąíh_w(x_i))^2] subject to per-partition validation MSE constraints; relaxed via slacks őĺ_q with penalty C, yielding a soft-constrained objective.
- Dual reformulation: Introduces Lagrange multipliers őľ (0‚Č§őľ‚Č§C1) and defines F(w,őľ,S)=‚ąĎ_{i‚ąąS}[őĽ||w||^2+(y_i‚ąíh_w(x_i))^2]+‚ąĎ_{q} őľ_q[(1/|V_q|)‚ąĎ_{j‚ąąV_q}(y_j‚ąíh_w(x_j))^2‚ąíőī]. Defines f(S)=F(w*(őľ*(S),S), őľ*(S), S). For convex losses, f(S)=g(S) (primal optimum); otherwise f is a lower bound.
- Theory: Shows f is monotone. Proves őĪ-submodularity for models with no bias, H-Lipschitz outputs, and bounded Hessian eigenvalues, with őĪ depending on őĽ, data bounds, and model constants; gives tighter bounds for linear models. Provides a unified bound on generalized curvature őļ.
- SELCON algorithm: Iterative majorization‚Äďminimization using semi-differential-inspired modular upper bounds tailored to őĪ-submodularity. At each iteration, builds a modular upper bound m(S) using values f(i|‚ąÖ) and f(i|Ňú\{i}) around current Ňú, then selects k items minimizing m. Works with estimated (possibly imperfect) (w,őľ) from a Train routine.
- Guarantees: With perfect training, approximation factor ‚Č§ k/[őĪŐā_f(1+(k‚ąí1)(1‚ąíőļŐā_f)őĪŐā_f)]. With imperfect training (error ‚Č§őĶ), adds a term 2kőĶ/‚Ąď, where ‚Ąď = min_i min_w [őĽ||w||^2+(y_i‚ąíh_w(x_i))^2]. Also proves NP-hardness of the original selection problems.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Datasets: Cadata (California housing), Law (LSAC bar passage study), NYSE-High, NYSE-Close (large stock time-series), Community-and-crime.
- Splits: 89% train, 1% validation, 10% test. For standard experiments Q=1 (single validation constraint). For fairness, validation partitions by protected race groups (Law: Q=8; Community-and-crime: Q=4).
- Models: Linear regression h_w(x)=w^T x; nonlinear two-layer model h_w(x)=w^T ReLU(Wx) with 5 hidden units.
- Hyperparameters/training: PyTorch + Adam. Epochs: N=2000 (Cadata, Law), N=5000 (NYSE datasets). Batch size b=min{|S|,1000}. SELCON uses short 3-epoch calls for marginal computations. őī set to 30% of mean validation error under Full-selection. C chosen to penalize slack (finite; exact value not specified). őĽ tuned per theoretical conditions (not explicitly listed per dataset).
- Baselines: Full-selection; Full-with-constraints; Random-selection; Random-with-constraints; CRAIG (coreset; stochastic variant for large data); GLISTER (validation-driven selection; updated every 35 epochs); SELCON-without-constraint.
- Metrics: Test MSE; computational efficiency measured as speed-up vs Full-selection. For fairness: mean absolute difference of squared errors across groups E[| (y_i‚ąíŇ∑_i)^2 ‚ąí (y_j‚ąíŇ∑_j)^2 | | i‚ąąV_q, j‚ąąV\V_q].
- Subset sizes: For smaller datasets (Cadata, Law): |S|/|D| in [1%, 10%]. For larger (NYSE): [0.1%, 1%].
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Scope limited to L2-regularized regression; extensions to classification and other losses not developed.
- Theoretical őĪ-submodularity and curvature guarantees require assumptions: no bias term, Lipschitz outputs, bounded Hessian; and sufficiently large őĽ (may be conservative).
- Approximation factor depends on response scale (can be O((y_max/y_min)^4)); mitigation via offsetting y is suggested but not intrinsic to the method.
- Minimization of f(S) is NP-hard; SELCON provides approximations and may involve substantial extra training calls to estimate marginal gains.
- Requires labeled validation sets and tuning of őī and C; performance sensitive to these choices.
- For non-convex models, dual f(S) lower-bounds the primal objective; mismatch may affect selection quality.
- CRAIG baseline omitted for some nonlinear large-scale settings due to scalability; broader comparisons for deep models are limited.
- Assumes fixed cardinality constraint; no exploration of alternative budget/weighting schemes (e.g., weighted coresets).
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Extend the formulation and theory to classification (e.g., logistic loss) and broader deep models, including bias terms and other regularizers.
- Develop tighter őĪ and curvature bounds and improved approximation ratios less sensitive to label scaling; investigate normalization/offset strategies theoretically.
- Design faster or streaming/online variants of SELCON (e.g., incremental marginal estimation, sketching) for very large datasets.
- Explore weighted coresets within the constrained-validation framework; hybrid selection-plus-weighting methods.
- Automatic or adaptive selection of őī and C, potentially via meta-learning or PAC-Bayes style generalization guarantees.
- Robustness and distribution shift: integrate adversarial or anchor regression constraints; domain or time-partitioned validation constraints.
- Fairness: richer group definitions, multiple simultaneous fairness metrics, and trade-offs among accuracy, efficiency, and fairness.
- Privacy-aware subset selection (DP constraints) with controlled validation error.
- Alternative constraints beyond cardinality (e.g., cost, diversity, routing) and multi-objective optimization.
- Improved modular upper bounds and semi-differential techniques for general őĪ-submodular minimization under imperfect or noisy objective estimates.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_code:
# Core SELCON subset selection and dualized validation constraint machinery
# Extracted from SELCON/model/SELCON.py, SELCON/model/Deep_SELCON.py,
# and the training wrappers in SELCON/linear.py and SELCON/deep.py,
# plus the simple models and dataset utilities they depend on.

import math
import numpy as np
import time
import copy
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, Dataset

# -------------------------------
# Minimal dataset wrappers
# -------------------------------
class CustomDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, data, target, device=None, transform=None):
        self.transform = transform
        if device is not None:
            self.data = torch.from_numpy(data.astype('float32')).to(device)
            self.targets = torch.from_numpy(target).to(device)
        else:
            self.data = data
            self.targets = target

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.targets)

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        if torch.is_tensor(idx):
            idx = idx.tolist()
        sample_data = self.data[idx]
        label = self.targets[idx]
        if self.transform is not None:
            sample_data = self.transform(sample_data)
        return (sample_data, label)

class CustomDataset_WithId(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, data, target, transform=None):
        self.transform = transform
        self.data = data
        self.targets = target
        self.X = self.data
        self.Y = self.targets

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.targets)

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        if torch.is_tensor(idx):
            idx = idx.tolist()
        sample_data = self.data[idx]
        label = self.targets[idx]
        if self.transform is not None:
            sample_data = self.transform(sample_data)
        return sample_data, label, idx

# -------------------------------
# Models (linear and shallow-deep)
# -------------------------------
class RegressionNet(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim):
        super(RegressionNet, self).__init__()
        self.linear = nn.Linear(input_dim, 1)
        self.feature_dim = input_dim
    def forward(self, x, last=False):
        scores = self.linear(x)
        if last:
            return scores.view(-1), x
        else:
            return scores.view(-1)
    def get_embedding_dim(self):
        return self.feature_dim

class TwoLayerNet(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim, hidden_units):
        super(TwoLayerNet, self).__init__()
        self.linear1 = nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_units)
        self.linear2 = nn.Linear(hidden_units, 1)
        self.feature_dim = hidden_units
    def forward(self, x, last=False):
        l1scores = F.relu(self.linear1(x))
        scores = self.linear2(l1scores)
        if last:
            return scores.view(-1), l1scores
        else:
            return scores.view(-1)
    def get_embedding_dim(self):
        return self.feature_dim

# -------------------------------
# SELCON (linear model): Training-loss objective with dualized validation constraint
# -------------------------------
class FindSubset_Vect_TrnLoss(object):
    def __init__(self, x_trn, y_trn, x_val, y_val, model, loss, device, delta, lr, lam, batch):
        self.x_trn = x_trn
        self.y_trn = y_trn
        self.x_val = x_val
        self.y_val = y_val
        self.model = model
        self.criterion = loss
        self.device = device
        self.delta = delta
        self.lr = lr
        self.lam = lam
        self.batch_size = batch
        torch.manual_seed(42)
        torch.cuda.manual_seed(42)
        np.random.seed(42)

    def precompute(self, f_pi_epoch, p_epoch, alphas):
        main_optimizer = torch.optim.Adam([{'params': self.model.parameters()}], lr=self.lr)
        dual_optimizer = torch.optim.Adam([{'params': alphas}], lr=self.lr)
        loader_val = DataLoader(CustomDataset(self.x_val, self.y_val, transform=None), shuffle=False, batch_size=self.batch_size)
        prev_loss = 1000
        stop_count = 0
        while True:
            main_optimizer.zero_grad()
            constraint = 0.
            for batch_idx in list(loader_val.batch_sampler):
                inputs, targets = loader_val.dataset[batch_idx]
                inputs, targets = inputs.to(self.device), targets.to(self.device)
                val_out = self.model(inputs)
                constraint += self.criterion(val_out, targets)
            constraint /= len(loader_val.batch_sampler)
            constraint = constraint - self.delta
            multiplier = alphas * constraint
            loss = multiplier
            self.F_phi = loss.item()
            loss.backward()
            main_optimizer.step()
            dual_optimizer.zero_grad()
            constraint = 0.
            for batch_idx in list(loader_val.batch_sampler):
                inputs, targets = loader_val.dataset[batch_idx]
                inputs, targets = inputs.to(self.device), targets.to(self.device)
                val_out = self.model(inputs)
                constraint += self.criterion(val_out, targets)
            constraint /= len(loader_val.batch_sampler)
            constraint = constraint - self.delta
            multiplier = -1.0 * alphas * constraint
            multiplier.backward()
            dual_optimizer.step()
            alphas.requires_grad = False
            alphas.clamp_(min=0.0)
            alphas.requires_grad = True
            if loss.item() <= 0.:
                break
            if abs(prev_loss - loss.item()) <= 1e-3 and stop_count >= 5:
                break
            elif abs(prev_loss - loss.item()) <= 1e-3:
                stop_count += 1
            else:
                stop_count = 0
            prev_loss = loss.item()
        if loss.item() <= 0.:
            alphas = torch.zeros_like(alphas)
        l = [torch.flatten(p) for p in self.model.state_dict().values()]
        flat = torch.cat(l).detach().clone()
        self.F_values = torch.zeros(len(self.x_trn), device=self.device)
        beta1, beta2 = main_optimizer.param_groups[0]['betas']
        loader_tr = DataLoader(CustomDataset_WithId(self.x_trn, self.y_trn, transform=None), shuffle=False, batch_size=self.batch_size*20)
        loader_val_big = DataLoader(CustomDataset(self.x_val, self.y_val, transform=None), shuffle=False, batch_size=self.batch_size*20)
        for batch_idx in list(loader_tr.batch_sampler):
            inputs, targets, idxs = loader_tr.dataset[batch_idx]
            inputs, targets = inputs.to(self.device), targets.to(self.device)
            ele_delta = self.delta.repeat(targets.shape[0]).to(self.device)
            weights = flat.view(1,-1).repeat(targets.shape[0], 1)
            ele_alphas = alphas.detach().repeat(targets.shape[0]).to(self.device)
            exp_avg_w = torch.zeros_like(weights)
            exp_avg_sq_w = torch.zeros_like(weights)
            exten_inp = torch.cat((inputs, torch.ones(inputs.shape[0], device=self.device).view(-1,1)), dim=1)
            bias_correction1 = 1.0
            bias_correction2 = 1.0
            step_size = self.lr
            for _ in range(p_epoch):
                trn_loss_g = torch.sum(exten_inp*weights, dim=1) - targets
                fin_trn_loss_g = exten_inp * 2 * trn_loss_g[:, None]
                weight_grad = fin_trn_loss_g + 2*self.lam * torch.cat((weights[:,:-1], torch.zeros((weights.shape[0],1), device=self.device)), dim=1)
                exp_avg_w.mul_(beta1).add_(1.0 - beta1, weight_grad)
                exp_avg_sq_w.mul_(beta2).addcmul_(1.0 - beta2, weight_grad, weight_grad)
                denom = exp_avg_sq_w.sqrt().add_(main_optimizer.param_groups[0]['eps'])
                bias_correction1 *= beta1
                bias_correction2 *= beta2
                step_size = step_size * math.sqrt(1.0-bias_correction2) / (1.0-bias_correction1)
                weights.addcdiv_(-step_size, exp_avg_w, denom)
            val_losses = 0.
            for batch_idx_val in list(loader_val_big.batch_sampler):
                inputs_val, targets_val = loader_val_big.dataset[batch_idx_val]
                inputs_val, targets_val = inputs_val.to(self.device), targets_val.to(self.device)
                exten_val = torch.cat((inputs_val, torch.ones(inputs_val.shape[0], device=self.device).view(-1,1)), dim=1)
                exten_val_y = torch.mean(targets_val).repeat(min(self.batch_size*20, targets.shape[0]))
                val_loss = torch.sum(weights*torch.mean(exten_val, dim=0), dim=1) - exten_val_y
                val_losses += val_loss*val_loss
            reg = torch.sum(weights[:,:-1]*weights[:,:-1], dim=1)
            trn_loss = torch.sum(exten_inp*weights, dim=1) - targets
            self.F_values[idxs] = trn_loss*trn_loss + self.lam*reg + torch.max(torch.zeros_like(ele_alphas), (val_losses/len(loader_val_big.batch_sampler)-ele_delta)*ele_alphas)

    def return_subset(self, theta_init, p_epoch, curr_subset, budget, batch, step, w_exp_avg, w_exp_avg_sq):
        m_values = self.F_values.detach().clone()
        self.model.load_state_dict(theta_init)
        loader_tr = DataLoader(CustomDataset_WithId(self.x_trn[curr_subset], self.y_trn[curr_subset], transform=None), shuffle=False, batch_size=batch)
        with torch.no_grad():
            F_curr = 0.
            sum_error = nn.MSELoss(reduction='sum')
            for batch_idx in list(loader_tr.batch_sampler):
                inputs, targets, _ = loader_tr.dataset[batch_idx]
                inputs, targets = inputs.to(self.device), targets.to(self.device)
                scores = self.model(inputs)
                F_curr += sum_error(scores, targets).item()
            l = [torch.flatten(p) for p in self.model.state_dict().values()]
            flatt = torch.cat(l)
            l2_reg = torch.sum(flatt[:-1]*flatt[:-1])
            F_curr += (self.lam * l2_reg * len(curr_subset)).item()
        main_optimizer = torch.optim.Adam([{'params': self.model.parameters()}], lr=self.lr)
        l = [torch.flatten(p) for p in self.model.state_dict().values()]
        flat = torch.cat(l).detach()
        beta1, beta2 = main_optimizer.param_groups[0]['betas']
        rem_len = (len(curr_subset)-1)
        b_idxs = 0
        device_new = self.device
        for batch_idx in list(loader_tr.batch_sampler):
            inputs, targets, _ = loader_tr.dataset[batch_idx]
            inputs, targets = inputs.to(self.device), targets.to(self.device)
            weights = flat.repeat(targets.shape[0], 1)
            exp_avg_w = w_exp_avg.repeat(targets.shape[0], 1)
            exp_avg_sq_w = w_exp_avg_sq.repeat(targets.shape[0], 1)
            exten_inp = torch.cat((inputs, torch.ones(inputs.shape[0], device=self.device).view(-1,1)), dim=1)
            bias_correction1 = beta1**step
            bias_correction2 = beta2**step
            for _ in range(p_epoch):
                sum_fin_trn_loss_g = torch.zeros_like(weights).to(device_new)
                for batch_idx_trn in list(loader_tr.batch_sampler):
                    inputs_trn, targets_trn, _ = loader_tr.dataset[batch_idx_trn]
                    inputs_trn, targets_trn = inputs_trn.to(self.device), targets_trn.to(self.device)
                    exten_trn = torch.cat((inputs_trn, torch.ones(inputs_trn.shape[0], device=self.device).view(-1,1)), dim=1).to(device_new)
                    exten_trn_y = targets_trn.view(-1,1).repeat(1, min(batch, targets.shape[0])).to(device_new)
                    sum_trn_loss_p = 2*(torch.matmul(exten_trn, torch.transpose(weights, 0, 1).to(device_new)) - exten_trn_y)
                    sum_fin_trn_loss_g += torch.sum(sum_trn_loss_p[:,:,None]*exten_trn[:,None,:], dim=0)
                    del exten_trn, exten_trn_y, sum_trn_loss_p, inputs_trn, targets_trn
                    torch.cuda.empty_cache()
                sum_fin_trn_loss_g = sum_fin_trn_loss_g.to(self.device)
                trn_loss_g = torch.sum(exten_inp*weights, dim=1) - targets
                fin_trn_loss_g = exten_inp*2*trn_loss_g[:,None]
                fin_trn_loss_g = (sum_fin_trn_loss_g - fin_trn_loss_g)/rem_len
                weight_grad = fin_trn_loss_g + 2*rem_len * torch.cat((weights[:,:-1], torch.zeros((weights.shape[0],1), device=self.device)), dim=1)
                exp_avg_w.mul_(beta1).add_(1.0 - beta1, weight_grad)
                exp_avg_sq_w.mul_(beta2).addcmul_(1.0 - beta2, weight_grad, weight_grad)
                denom = exp_avg_sq_w.sqrt().add_(main_optimizer.param_groups[0]['eps'])
                step_size = (self.lr) * math.sqrt(1.0-bias_correction2) / (1.0-bias_correction1)
                weights.addcdiv_(-step_size, exp_avg_w, denom)
            reg = torch.sum(weights[:,:-1]*weights[:,:-1], dim=1)
            trn_losses = 0.
            for batch_idx_trn in list(loader_tr.batch_sampler):
                inputs_trn, targets_trn, _ = loader_tr.dataset[batch_idx_trn]
                inputs_trn, targets_trn = inputs_trn.to(self.device), targets_trn.to(self.device)
                exten_trn = torch.cat((inputs_trn, torch.ones(inputs_trn.shape[0], device=self.device).view(-1,1)), dim=1)
                exten_trn_y = targets_trn.view(-1,1).repeat(1, min(batch, targets.shape[0]))
                trn_loss = torch.matmul(exten_trn, torch.transpose(weights, 0, 1)) - exten_trn_y
                trn_losses += torch.sum(trn_loss*trn_loss, dim=0)
            trn_loss_ind = torch.sum(exten_inp*weights, dim=1) - targets
            trn_losses -= trn_loss_ind*trn_loss_ind
            abs_value = F_curr - (trn_losses + self.lam*reg*rem_len)
            neg_ind = ((abs_value) < 0).nonzero().view(-1)
            abs_value[neg_ind] = torch.max(self.F_values)
            m_values[torch.tensor(curr_subset)[b_idxs*batch:(b_idxs+1)*batch]] = abs_value
            b_idxs += 1
        _, indices = m_values.topk(budget, largest=False)
        return list(indices.cpu().numpy())

# Variant that approximates validation via average features (kept for completeness)
class FindSubset_Vect_No_ValLoss(object):
    def __init__(self, x_trn, y_trn, x_val, y_val, model, loss, device, delta, lr, lam, batch):
        self.x_trn = x_trn
        self.y_trn = y_trn
        self.x_val = x_val
        self.y_val = y_val
        self.model = model
        self.criterion = loss
        self.device = device
        self.delta = delta
        self.lr = lr
        self.lam = lam
        self.batch_size = batch
        torch.manual_seed(42)
        torch.cuda.manual_seed(42)
        np.random.seed(42)
    # precompute and return_subset implement a similar pipeline to TrnLoss with averaged val features
    # (omitted here for brevity as TrnLoss is the main variant used in the training loop)

# -------------------------------
# SELCON for shallow-deep model (uses penultimate features to linearize)
# -------------------------------
class FindSubset_Vect_TrnLoss_Deep(object):
    def __init__(self, x_trn, y_trn, x_val, y_val, model, loss, device, delta, lr, lam, batch):
        self.x_trn = x_trn
        self.y_trn = y_trn
        self.x_val = x_val
        self.y_val = y_val
        self.model = model
        self.criterion = loss
        self.device = device
        self.delta = delta
        self.lr = lr
        self.lam = lam
        self.batch_size = batch
        torch.manual_seed(42)
        torch.cuda.manual_seed(42)
        np.random.seed(42)

    def precompute(self, f_pi_epoch, p_epoch, alphas):
        main_optimizer = torch.optim.Adam([{'params': self.model.parameters()}], lr=self.lr)
        dual_optimizer = torch.optim.Adam([{'params': alphas}], lr=self.lr)
        loader_val = DataLoader(CustomDataset(self.x_val, self.y_val, transform=None), shuffle=False, batch_size=self.batch_size)
        prev_loss = 1000
        stop_count = 0
        while True:
            main_optimizer.zero_grad()
            constraint = 0.
            for batch_idx in list(loader_val.batch_sampler):
                inputs, targets = loader_val.dataset[batch_idx]
                inputs, targets = inputs.to(self.device), targets.to(self.device)
                val_out = self.model(inputs)
                constraint += self.criterion(val_out, targets)
            constraint /= len(loader_val.batch_sampler)
            constraint = constraint - self.delta
            multiplier = alphas * constraint
            loss = multiplier
            self.F_phi = loss.item()
            loss.backward()
            main_optimizer.step()
            dual_optimizer.zero_grad()
            constraint = 0.
            for batch_idx in list(loader_val.batch_sampler):
                inputs, targets = loader_val.dataset[batch_idx]
                inputs, targets = inputs.to(self.device), targets.to(self.device)
                val_out = self.model(inputs)
                constraint += self.criterion(val_out, targets)
            constraint /= len(loader_val.batch_sampler)
            constraint = constraint - self.delta
            multiplier = -1.0 * alphas * constraint
            multiplier.backward()
            dual_optimizer.step()
            alphas.requires_grad = False
            alphas.clamp_(min=0.0)
            alphas.requires_grad = True
            if loss.item() <= 0.:
                break
            if abs(prev_loss - loss.item()) <= 1e-3 and stop_count >= 5:
                break
            elif abs(prev_loss - loss.item()) <= 1e-3:
                stop_count += 1
            else:
                stop_count = 0
            prev_loss = loss.item()
        if loss.item() <= 0.:
            alphas = torch.zeros_like(alphas)
        l = [torch.flatten(p) for p in self.model.parameters()]
        self.old_flat = torch.cat(l[2:]).detach().clone()
        self.old_alphas = alphas.detach().clone()

    def update_F_values(self, p_epoch, mean_val_x, mean_val_y):
        main_optimizer = torch.optim.Adam([{'params': self.model.parameters()}], lr=self.lr)
        step_size = self.lr
        beta1, beta2 = main_optimizer.param_groups[0]['betas']
        self.F_values = torch.zeros(len(self.x_trn), device=self.device)
        loader_tr_ful = DataLoader(CustomDataset_WithId(self.x_trn, self.y_trn, transform=None), shuffle=False, batch_size=self.batch_size*20)
        for batch_idx_trn in list(loader_tr_ful.batch_sampler):
            inputs, targets, _ = loader_tr_ful.dataset[batch_idx_trn]
            inputs, targets = inputs.to(self.device), targets.to(self.device)
            with torch.no_grad():
                out, l1 = self.model(inputs, last=True)
            if batch_idx_trn[0] == 0:
                mod_x_trn = l1
                mod_y_trn = targets
            else:
                mod_x_trn = torch.cat((mod_x_trn, l1), dim=0)
                mod_y_trn = torch.cat((mod_y_trn, targets), dim=0)
        loader_tr = DataLoader(CustomDataset_WithId(mod_x_trn, mod_y_trn, transform=None), shuffle=False, batch_size=self.batch_size*50)
        for batch_idx in list(loader_tr.batch_sampler):
            inputs, targets, idxs = loader_tr.dataset[batch_idx]
            inputs, targets = inputs.to(self.device), targets.to(self.device)
            ele_delta = self.delta.repeat(targets.shape[0]).to(self.device)
            weights = self.old_flat.view(1,-1).repeat(targets.shape[0], 1)
            ele_alphas = self.old_alphas.detach().repeat(targets.shape[0]).to(self.device)
            exp_avg_w = torch.zeros_like(weights)
            exp_avg_sq_w = torch.zeros_like(weights)
            exten_inp = torch.cat((inputs, torch.ones(inputs.shape[0], device=self.device).view(-1,1)), dim=1)
            bias_correction1 = 1.0
            bias_correction2 = 1.0
            for _ in range(p_epoch):
                trn_loss_g = torch.sum(exten_inp*weights, dim=1) - targets
                fin_trn_loss_g = exten_inp*2*trn_loss_g[:, None]
                weight_grad = fin_trn_loss_g + 2*self.lam * torch.cat((weights[:,:-1], torch.zeros((weights.shape[0],1), device=self.device)), dim=1)
                exp_avg_w.mul_(beta1).add_(1.0 - beta1, weight_grad)
                exp_avg_sq_w.mul_(beta2).addcmul_(1.0 - beta2, weight_grad, weight_grad)
                denom = exp_avg_sq_w.sqrt().add_(main_optimizer.param_groups[0]['eps'])
                bias_correction1 *= beta1
                bias_correction2 *= beta2
                step_size = step_size * math.sqrt(1.0-bias_correction2) / (1.0-bias_correction1)
                weights.addcdiv_(-step_size, exp_avg_w, denom)
            reg = torch.sum(weights[:,:-1]*weights[:,:-1], dim=1)
            trn_loss = torch.sum(exten_inp*weights, dim=1) - targets
            val_loss = torch.matmul(weights[:,:-1], mean_val_x) + weights[:,-1] - mean_val_y
            self.F_values[idxs] = trn_loss*trn_loss + self.lam*reg + torch.max(torch.zeros_like(ele_alphas), (val_loss*val_loss - ele_delta) * ele_alphas)

    def return_subset(self, theta_init, p_epoch, curr_subset, budget, batch, step, w_exp_avg, w_exp_avg_sq):
        self.model.load_state_dict(theta_init)
        loader_tr_ful = DataLoader(CustomDataset_WithId(self.x_trn[curr_subset], self.y_trn[curr_subset], transform=None), shuffle=False, batch_size=batch)
        loader_val = DataLoader(CustomDataset(self.x_val, self.y_val, transform=None), shuffle=False, batch_size=batch)
        sum_error = nn.MSELoss(reduction='sum')
        for batch_idx_trn in list(loader_tr_ful.batch_sampler):
            inputs, targets, _ = loader_tr_ful.dataset[batch_idx_trn]
            inputs, targets = inputs.to(self.device), targets.to(self.device)
            with torch.no_grad():
                out, l1 = self.model(inputs, last=True)
            if batch_idx_trn[0] == 0:
                mod_x_trn = l1
                mod_y_trn = targets
            else:
                mod_x_trn = torch.cat((mod_x_trn, l1), dim=0)
                mod_y_trn = torch.cat((mod_y_trn, targets), dim=0)
        for batch_idx_val in list(loader_val.batch_sampler):
            inputs_val, targets_val = loader_val.dataset[batch_idx_val]
            inputs_val, targets_val = inputs_val.to(self.device), targets_val.to(self.device)
            with torch.no_grad():
                out, l1 = self.model(inputs_val, last=True)
            if batch_idx_val[0] == 0:
                mod_x_val = l1
                mod_y_val = targets_val
            else:
                mod_x_val = torch.cat((mod_x_val, l1), dim=0)
                mod_y_val = torch.cat((mod_y_val, targets_val), dim=0)
        loader_val_mod = DataLoader(CustomDataset(mod_x_val, mod_y_val, transform=None), shuffle=False, batch_size=batch)
        mean_x_val = torch.mean(mod_x_val, dim=0)
        mean_y_val = torch.mean(mod_y_val)
        self.update_F_values(p_epoch, mean_x_val, mean_y_val)
        m_values = self.F_values.detach().clone()
        with torch.no_grad():
            F_curr = 0.
            for batch_idx in list(loader_tr_ful.batch_sampler):
                inputs, targets, _ = loader_tr_ful.dataset[batch_idx]
                inputs, targets = inputs.to(self.device), targets.to(self.device)
                scores = self.model(inputs)
                F_curr += sum_error(scores, targets).item()
            l = [torch.flatten(p) for p in self.model.parameters()]
            flatt = torch.cat(l[2:])
            l2_reg = torch.sum(flatt[:-1]*flatt[:-1])
            F_curr += (self.lam * l2_reg * len(curr_subset)).item()
        main_optimizer = torch.optim.Adam([{'params': self.model.parameters()}], lr=self.lr)
        l = [torch.flatten(p) for p in self.model.parameters()]
        flat = torch.cat(l[2:]).detach()
        beta1, beta2 = main_optimizer.param_groups[0]['betas']
        rem_len = (len(curr_subset)-1)
        b_idxs = 0
        device_new = self.device
        loader_tr = DataLoader(CustomDataset_WithId(mod_x_trn, mod_y_trn, transform=None), shuffle=False, batch_size=batch)
        for batch_idx in list(loader_tr.batch_sampler):
            inputs, targets, _ = loader_tr.dataset[batch_idx]
            inputs, targets = inputs.to(self.device), targets.to(self.device)
            weights = flat.repeat(targets.shape[0], 1)
            exp_avg_w = w_exp_avg.repeat(targets.shape[0], 1)
            exp_avg_sq_w = w_exp_avg_sq.repeat(targets.shape[0], 1)
            exten_inp = torch.cat((inputs, torch.ones(inputs.shape[0], device=self.device).view(-1,1)), dim=1)
            bias_correction1 = beta1**step
            bias_correction2 = beta2**step
            for _ in range(p_epoch):
                sum_fin_trn_loss_g = torch.zeros_like(weights).to(device_new)
                for batch_idx_trn in list(loader_tr.batch_sampler):
                    inputs_trn, targets_trn, _ = loader_tr.dataset[batch_idx_trn]
                    inputs_trn, targets_trn = inputs_trn.to(self.device), targets_trn.to(self.device)
                    exten_trn = torch.cat((inputs_trn, torch.ones(inputs_trn.shape[0], device=self.device).view(-1,1)), dim=1).to(device_new)
                    exten_trn_y = targets_trn.view(-1,1).repeat(1, min(batch, targets.shape[0])).to(device_new)
                    sum_trn_loss_p = 2*(torch.matmul(exten_trn, torch.transpose(weights, 0, 1).to(device_new)) - exten_trn_y)
                    sum_fin_trn_loss_g += torch.sum(sum_trn_loss_p[:,:,None]*exten_trn[:,None,:], dim=0)
                    del exten_trn, exten_trn_y, sum_trn_loss_p, inputs_trn, targets_trn
                    torch.cuda.empty_cache()
                sum_fin_trn_loss_g = sum_fin_trn_loss_g.to(self.device)
                trn_loss_g = torch.sum(exten_inp*weights, dim=1) - targets
                fin_trn_loss_g = exten_inp*2*trn_loss_g[:,None]
                fin_trn_loss_g = (sum_fin_trn_loss_g - fin_trn_loss_g)/rem_len
                weight_grad = fin_trn_loss_g + 2*rem_len * torch.cat((weights[:,:-1], torch.zeros((weights.shape[0],1), device=self.device)), dim=1)
                exp_avg_w.mul_(beta1).add_(1.0 - beta1, weight_grad)
                exp_avg_sq_w.mul_(beta2).addcmul_(1.0 - beta2, weight_grad, weight_grad)
                denom = exp_avg_sq_w.sqrt().add_(main_optimizer.param_groups[0]['eps'])
                step_size = (self.lr) * math.sqrt(1.0-bias_correction2) / (1.0-bias_correction1)
                weights.addcdiv_(-step_size, exp_avg_w, denom)
            reg = torch.sum(weights[:,:-1]*weights[:,:-1], dim=1)
            trn_losses = 0.
            for batch_idx_trn in list(loader_tr.batch_sampler):
                inputs_trn, targets_trn, _ = loader_tr.dataset[batch_idx_trn]
                inputs_trn, targets_trn = inputs_trn.to(self.device), targets_trn.to(self.device)
                exten_trn = torch.cat((inputs_trn, torch.ones(inputs_trn.shape[0], device=self.device).view(-1,1)), dim=1)
                exten_trn_y = targets_trn.view(-1,1).repeat(1, min(batch, targets.shape[0]))
                trn_loss = torch.matmul(exten_trn, torch.transpose(weights, 0, 1)) - exten_trn_y
                trn_losses += torch.sum(trn_loss*trn_loss, dim=0)
            trn_loss_ind = torch.sum(exten_inp*weights, dim=1) - targets
            trn_losses -= trn_loss_ind*trn_loss_ind
            abs_value = F_curr - (trn_losses + self.lam*reg*rem_len)
            neg_ind = ((abs_value) < 0).nonzero().view(-1)
            abs_value[neg_ind] = torch.max(self.F_values)
            m_values[torch.tensor(curr_subset)[b_idxs*batch:(b_idxs+1)*batch]] = abs_value
            b_idxs += 1
        _, indices = m_values.topk(budget, largest=False)
        return list(indices.cpu().numpy())

# (Optional) multi-partition variant for fairness constraints across multiple validation slices
class FindSubset_Vect_Fair(object):
    def __init__(self, x_trn, y_trn, x_val_list, y_val_list, model, loss, device, delta, lr, lam, batch):
        self.x_trn = x_trn
        self.y_trn = y_trn
        self.x_val_list = x_val_list
        self.y_val_list = y_val_list
        self.model = model
        self.criterion = loss
        self.device = device
        self.delta = delta
        self.lr = lr
        self.dual_lr = 0.1
        self.lam = lam
        self.batch_size = batch
        torch.manual_seed(42)
        torch.cuda.manual_seed(42)
        np.random.seed(42)
    # precompute() and return_subset() implement alternating primal-dual updates of (w, alphas) across partitions
    # and the same modular upper-bound construction as above (full code present in SELCON/model/Fair_SELCON.py)

# -------------------------------
# Training wrappers that interleave training with SELCON subset updates
# -------------------------------
class RegressionExperiment:
    def __init__(self):
        self.select_every = 35
        self.reg_lambda = 1e-5
        self.batch_size = 4000
        self.learning_rate = 0.01
        self.device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
        self.psuedo_length = 1.0
        self.subset_idx = None
        torch.manual_seed(42)
        np.random.seed(42)

    def weight_reset(self, m):
        torch.manual_seed(42)
        if type(m) == nn.Linear:
            torch.nn.init.xavier_uniform_(m.weight)
            m.bias.data.fill_(0.1)

    def train_model(self, x_trn, y_trn, x_val, y_val, fraction, delt=[0.3], num_epochs=2000):
        sub_epoch = 3
        N, M = x_trn.shape
        bud = int(fraction * N)
        train_batch_size = min(bud, 1000)
        deltas = torch.tensor(delt).to(self.device)
        rand_idxs = list(np.random.choice(N, size=bud, replace=False))
        idxs = rand_idxs
        criterion = nn.MSELoss()
        model = RegressionNet(M)
        model.apply(self.weight_reset)
        model = model.to(self.device)
        optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=self.learning_rate)
        scheduler = optim.lr_scheduler.StepLR(optimizer, step_size=1, gamma=0.1)
        idxs.sort()
        np.random.seed(42)
        np_sub_idxs = np.array(idxs)
        np.random.shuffle(np_sub_idxs)
        loader_tr = DataLoader(CustomDataset(x_trn[np_sub_idxs], y_trn[np_sub_idxs], transform=None), shuffle=False, batch_size=train_batch_size)
        loader_val = DataLoader(CustomDataset(x_val, y_val, transform=None), shuffle=False, batch_size=self.batch_size)
        cached_state_dict = copy.deepcopy(model.state_dict())
        if self.psuedo_length == 1.0:
            sub_rand_idxs = [s for s in range(N)]
            current_idxs = idxs
        else:
            sub_rand_idxs = [s for s in range(N)]
            new_ele = set(sub_rand_idxs).difference(set(idxs))
            sub_rand_idxs = list(np.random.choice(list(new_ele), size=int(self.psuedo_length*N), replace=False))
            sub_rand_idxs = idxs + sub_rand_idxs
            current_idxs = [s for s in range(len(idxs))]
        fsubset = FindSubset_Vect_TrnLoss(x_trn[sub_rand_idxs], y_trn[sub_rand_idxs], x_val, y_val, model, criterion, self.device, deltas, self.learning_rate, self.reg_lambda, self.batch_size)
        fsubset.precompute(int(num_epochs/4), sub_epoch, torch.randn_like(deltas, device=self.device))
        model.load_state_dict(cached_state_dict)
        prev_loss = 1000
        prev_loss2 = 1000
        mul = 1
        lr_count = 0
        for i in range(num_epochs):
            temp_loss = 0.
            for batch_idx in list(loader_tr.batch_sampler):
                inputs, targets = loader_tr.dataset[batch_idx]
                inputs, targets = inputs.to(self.device), targets.to(self.device)
                optimizer.zero_grad()
                scores = model(inputs)
                l2_reg = 0
                for p in model.parameters():
                    l2_reg += torch.norm(p)
                loss = criterion(scores, targets) + self.reg_lambda * l2_reg * len(batch_idx)
                temp_loss += loss.item()
                loss.backward()
                for p in filter(lambda p: p.grad is not None, model.parameters()):
                    p.grad.data.clamp_(min=-.1, max=.1)
                optimizer.step()
            if ((i + 1) % self.select_every == 0):
                cached_state_dict = copy.deepcopy(model.state_dict())
                clone_dict = copy.deepcopy(cached_state_dict)
                fsubset.lr = optimizer.param_groups[0]['lr'] * mul
                state_values = list(optimizer.state.values())
                step = state_values[0]['step'] if len(state_values)>0 else 0
                if len(state_values) >= 2:
                    w_exp_avg = torch.cat((state_values[0]['exp_avg'].view(-1), state_values[1]['exp_avg']))
                    w_exp_avg_sq = torch.cat((state_values[0]['exp_avg_sq'].view(-1), state_values[1]['exp_avg_sq']))
                else:
                    w_exp_avg = torch.zeros(M+1, device=self.device)
                    w_exp_avg_sq = torch.zeros(M+1, device=self.device)
                d_sub_idxs = fsubset.return_subset(clone_dict, sub_epoch, current_idxs, bud, train_batch_size, step, w_exp_avg, w_exp_avg_sq)
                current_idxs = d_sub_idxs
                d_sub_idxs = list(np.array(sub_rand_idxs)[d_sub_idxs])
                new_ele = set(d_sub_idxs).difference(set(idxs))
                if len(new_ele) > 0.1*bud:
                    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam([{'params': model.parameters()}], lr=optimizer.param_groups[0]['lr']*mul)
                    lr_count = 0
                idxs = d_sub_idxs
                idxs.sort()
                np.random.seed(42)
                np_sub_idxs = np.array(idxs)
                np.random.shuffle(np_sub_idxs)
                loader_tr = DataLoader(CustomDataset(x_trn[np_sub_idxs], y_trn[np_sub_idxs], transform=None), shuffle=False, batch_size=train_batch_size)
                model.load_state_dict(cached_state_dict)
            if abs(prev_loss - temp_loss) <= 1e-1*mul or prev_loss2 == temp_loss:
                lr_count += 1
                if lr_count == 10:
                    scheduler.step()
                    mul /= 10
                    lr_count = 0
            else:
                lr_count = 0
            prev_loss2 = prev_loss
            prev_loss = temp_loss
        self.subset_idx = idxs
        self.model = model
        self.loader_val = loader_val
        return idxs

class DeepSelectionExperiment:
    def __init__(self):

        self.reg_lambda = 1e-5
        self.batch_size = 4000

        fsubset.precompute(int(num_epochs/4), sub_epoch, torch.randn_like(deltas, device=self.device))

        for i in range(num_epochs):
            temp_loss = 0.
            for batch_idx in list(loader_tr.batch_sampler):
                inputs, targets = loader_tr.dataset[batch_idx]
                inputs, targets = inputs.to(self.device), targets.to(self.device)
                optimizer.zero_grad()
                scores = model(inputs)
                l2_reg = 0
                for p in model.parameters():
                    l2_reg += torch.norm(p)
                loss = criterion(scores, targets) + self.reg_lambda * l2_reg * len(batch_idx)
                temp_loss += loss.item()
                loss.backward()
                for p in filter(lambda p: p.grad is not None, model.parameters()):
                    p.grad.data.clamp_(min=-.1, max=.1)
                optimizer.step()
            if ((i + 1) % self.select_every == 0):
                cached_state_dict = copy.deepcopy(model.state_dict())
                clone_dict = copy.deepcopy(cached_state_dict)
                fsubset.lr = min(optimizer.param_groups[0]['lr']*mul, 1e-6)
                step = 0
                w_exp_avg = torch.zeros(self.hidden_units+1, device=self.device)
                w_exp_avg_sq = torch.zeros(self.hidden_units+1, device=self.device)
                d_sub_idxs = fsubset.return_subset(clone_dict, sub_epoch, current_idxs, bud, self.batch_size, step, w_exp_avg, w_exp_avg_sq)
                current_idxs = d_sub_idxs
                d_sub_idxs = list(np.array(sub_rand_idxs)[d_sub_idxs])

llm_extracted_info.experimental_info:
Core objective and dual reformulation:
- Optimize over subset S (|S| = k) and model parameters w to minimize sum_{i in S} [lambda ||w||^2 + (y_i - h_w(x_i))^2] under per-partition validation MSE constraints E_{j in V_q}[(y_j - h_w(x_j))^2] <= delta.
- Relax constraints via Lagrange multipliers mu_q >= 0 (implemented as nonnegative alphas), forming the soft-constrained objective F(w, mu, S) = sum_{i in S} [lambda ||w||^2 + (y_i - h_w(x_i))^2] + sum_q mu_q (E_{j in V_q}[val loss] - delta).
- With convex losses, f(S) = min_w max_mu F equals the primal optimum; otherwise a lower bound.

SELCON selection algorithm (used in both linear and shallow-deep experiments):
- Train the model on the current subset with Adam.
- Every select_every epochs (default 35), build a modular upper bound m(S) of f(S) tailored to alpha-submodularity by estimating per-element marginal contributions with a fast, vectorized, Adam-like inner-loop update (p_epoch inner steps) for each candidate element.
- Return the k items with smallest m-values and continue training on the updated subset.
- Dual variables (alphas) for validation constraint are updated by gradient ascent (clamped to >= 0) during a precompute stage, interleaved with primal updates to model parameters on validation batches.

Key components and where they are implemented:
- Linear SELCON: FindSubset_Vect_TrnLoss (in SELCON/model/SELCON.py) implements precompute() (alternating primal-dual updates on validation) and return_subset() (modular upper bound via vectorized Adam simulation over candidates).
- Shallow-deep SELCON: FindSubset_Vect_TrnLoss_Deep (in SELCON/model/Deep_SELCON.py) performs the same but linearizes around the penultimate features of a TwoLayerNet for efficient per-item evaluation.
- Multi-partition constraints: FindSubset_Vect_Fair (in SELCON/model/Fair_SELCON.py) handles multiple validation slices by maintaining a vector of dual variables, one per partition.
- Training wrappers: RegressionExperiment (linear) and DeepSelectionExperiment (two-layer) interleave standard training and SELCON selection.

Default hyperparameters (from linear.py and deep.py):
- Optimizer: Adam; learning_rate = 0.01; StepLR scheduler with step_size = 1 epoch, gamma = 0.1.
- Regularization: L2 penalty aggregated as sum of parameter norms (or squared for some variants) multiplied by reg_lambda and by batch size; reg_lambda = 1e-5.
- Loss: Mean Squared Error.
- Gradient clipping: clamp parameter gradients to [-0.1, 0.1].
- Selection schedule: select_every = 35 epochs.
- Inner selection iterations: sub_epoch = 3 (p_epoch in code), i.e., 3 Adam-like updates per candidate to approximate marginals.
- Precompute (primal-dual warm-start): int(num_epochs/4) outer steps alternating primal and dual updates on validation.
- Dual variable initialization: alphas initialized as torch.randn_like(delta), clamped to be non-negative after each dual step. Single validation partition uses scalar delta; multi-partition uses a vector of deltas.
- Device and seeds: device = CUDA if available else CPU; torch.manual_seed(42); numpy.random.seed(42).

Batching and budgets:
- Let N = number of training examples, M = feature dimension; budget k = fraction * N.
- Train mini-batch size: min(k, 1000).
- Validation/test batch size: 4000.
- Pseudo-length: 1.0 by default (use all remaining candidates for scoring). If < 1.0, append a random subset of non-selected examples to current subset when scoring.

Models:
- Linear: RegressionNet(M) with a single linear layer (bias included).
- Shallow-deep: TwoLayerNet(M, hidden_units=5) with ReLU hidden layer and linear output; selection uses its penultimate layer as features for efficient linearized updates.

Stopping and LR schedule details:
- Learning rate decay trigger: reduce LR (via StepLR) when the absolute change in per-epoch training loss is small for 10 checks; multiplier tracked via `mul` that scales the Adam step used in subset scoring.

Validation constraint specifics:
- delta (őī) is a scalar (single validation set) or vector (per-partition) threshold for mean squared error.
- In precompute(), the validation loss is averaged over validation mini-batches and compared against őī to update alphas via gradient ascent. Alphas are clamped to be nonnegative each step to enforce mu_q >= 0.

Outputs captured post-training:
- subset_idx: indices of the final selected subset.
- For evaluation (outside this extract), validation and test MSE are computed by iterating over loaders and aggregating per-example squared errors.

Typical experimental tuples (as used in older run scripts):
- Fractions (k/N): {0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07, 0.10} for tabular; {0.001, 0.003, 0.005, 0.007, 0.01} for time-series.
- Deltas: e.g., 0.3 for many regression datasets; for LawSchool 0.04; for time-series 1.0. Experiments may sweep deltas to build trade-off curves.
- Epochs: linear up to 2000; deep up to 5000.

Notes on modular upper bound construction:
- The return_subset() methods compute m_values as F_values (per-element upper bounds) corrected by an estimate of marginal reduction from removing each candidate, using vectorized Adam-like updates of per-candidate replicated weights. This implements the SELCON majorization-minimization step that is tailored to alpha-submodular objectives and uses the estimated (w, mu) from the current Train routine.

research_study_list.title: Towards a statistical theory of data selection under weak supervision
research_study_list.abstract:
Given a sample of size $N$, it is often useful to select a subsample of
smaller size $n<N$ to be used for statistical estimation or learning. Such a
data selection step is useful to reduce the requirements of data labeling and
the computational complexity of learning. We assume to be given $N$ unlabeled
samples $\{{\boldsymbol x}_i\}_{i\le N}$, and to be given access to a
`surrogate model' that can predict labels $y_i$ better than random guessing.
Our goal is to select a subset of the samples, to be denoted by $\{{\boldsymbol
x}_i\}_{i\in G}$, of size $|G|=n<N$. We then acquire labels for this set and we
use them to train a model via regularized empirical risk minimization.
  By using a mixture of numerical experiments on real and synthetic data, and
mathematical derivations under low- and high- dimensional asymptotics, we show
that: $(i)$~Data selection can be very effective, in particular beating
training on the full sample in some cases; $(ii)$~Certain popular choices in
data selection methods (e.g. unbiased reweighted subsampling, or influence
function-based subsampling) can be substantially suboptimal.
research_study_list.full_text: Towards a statistical theory of data selection under weak supervision Germain Kolossov‚ąó Andrea Montanari‚ąó Pulkit Tandon‚ąó October 5, 2023 Abstract Given a sample of size N, it is often useful to select a subsample of smaller size n < Nto be used for statistical estimation or learning. Such a data selection step is useful to reduce the requirements of data labeling and the computational complexity of learning. We assume to be given N unlabeled samples {xi}i‚Č§N, and to be given access to a ‚Äėsurrogate model‚Äô that can predict labels yi better than random guessing. Our goal is to select a subset of the samples, to be denoted by {xi}i‚ąąG, of size |G| = n < N. We then acquire labels for this set and we use them to train a model via regularized empirical risk minimization. By using a mixture of numerical experiments on real and synthetic data, and mathematical derivations under low- and high- dimensional asymptotics, we show that: ( i) Data selection can be very effective, in particular beating training on the full sample in some cases; ( ii) Certain popular choices in data selection methods (e.g. unbiased reweighted subsampling, or influence function-based subsampling) can be substantially suboptimal.
