llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces Cluster-Margin, a scalable batch active learning algorithm that combines uncertainty (margin-based) and diversity (hierarchical agglomerative clustering with round-robin sampling). Demonstrates scalability to extremely large batch sizes (100K‚Äď1M) with substantial label-efficiency gains over state-of-the-art baselines on the Open Images v6 dataset (up to 60% fewer labels than the next best method at 1M batch size) and competitive performance on CIFAR10/100 and SVHN. Provides an initial theoretical analysis via a related algorithm (Cluster-MarginV) establishing label complexity improvements by a factor ő≤, with concrete ő≤ = d/log(k) under certain assumptions, and shows approximate connections to Cluster-Margin in specific settings.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: Cluster-Margin pipeline: (1) Seed: sample a random seed set P, request labels, and train model f. (2) Embedding & clustering: embed all unlabeled data X using the penultimate layer of f; run Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering (HAC) with average linkage once on X using distance threshold őĶ to form clusters C (HAC O(n^2 log n), multi-threaded). (3) Iterative selection: at each iteration, compute margin scores (difference between top-2 class probabilities) for all unlabeled points; select the km lowest-margin examples M; map M to clusters; sort clusters ascending by size; perform round-robin random sampling across clusters to pick a diverse subset of size kt (kt ‚Č§ km), cycling back to the smallest unsaturated cluster to maximize cluster coverage. Typical setting km = 10 kt; őĶ chosen so average cluster size allows exhausting clusters. Per-iteration sampling is O(n log n); BADGE and CoreSet are O(k n). Multi-label adaptation (Open Images): sample k images via Cluster-Margin, then uniformly sample k image‚Äďclass pairs from all candidate pairs present within those images. Baselines: Margin (uncertainty by smallest top-2 probability gap), BADGE (k-MEANS++ on gradient embeddings gx composed of l blocks with gx(y) = (p(y|x) ‚ąí 1_{y=Ň∑1}) z_x), and CoreSet (greedy 2-approx k-center on embeddings). Scalability enhancements: multi-round HAC and a cluster-assignment variant that clusters only the seed embeddings and assigns the rest by nearest centroid within őĶc. Theory (Cluster-MarginV): uniformly sample points in the low-margin region w.r.t. a consistent hypothesis, then apply a ő≤-efficient volume-based sampler to shrink version-space diameter; yields label complexity O(ő≤(d + log(1/őī) + log log(1/őĶ)) log(1/őĶ)). Shows ő≤ = d/log(k) for a specific hypothesis class and distributions and an ‚Ą¶(1/log k) lower bound for any sampler.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: Large-scale: Open Images v6 (multi-label; ~9.01M train images, 19,957 classes, partial annotations; avg ~6 classes annotated per image). Evaluation metric: pooled Average Precision (AP). Model: ResNet-101 (tf-slim), global pooling + 128-d embedding layer; training with 64 Cloud TPU v4s (2 cores each), effective batch size 6144; initial lr 1e-4 with logarithmic decay every 5√ó10^8 examples. Fine-tuning setup: pretrain on validation split for 150K SGD steps; seed 300K image‚Äďclass pairs, train 15K steps; then 10 active learning iterations with batch sizes 100K or 1M image‚Äďclass pairs per iteration, training 15K steps after each; 3 trials per method. Cluster-Margin parameters: km = 10 kt; őĶ picked so average cluster size ‚Č• 10. Multi-label sampling: first select k images, then uniformly sample k image‚Äďclass pairs from candidates within selected images. Baseline scaling: for BADGE/CoreSet, partition unlabeled pool to meet time constraints (n‚Čą9M; for 100K: BADGE 20 partitions, CoreSet 1; for 1M: BADGE 200, CoreSet 20). Gradient embedding dimension d=256 for Open Images (l=2 blocks √ó 128-d embedding). Results: Cluster-Margin achieves target pooled AP with 29% fewer labels than Margin at 100K and ~60% fewer at 1M; relative to final pooled AP of baselines, Cluster-Margin needs 66% (BADGE), 53% (CoreSet), 71% (Margin), 52% (Random) of labels at 100K; and 38%, 40%, 37%, 35% respectively at 1M. Small-scale: CIFAR10 (50K train/10K test/10 classes), CIFAR100 (50K/10K/100 classes), SVHN (73,257 train/26,032 test/10 classes). Model: VGG16 (tf-keras) with ImageNet initialization; SGD lr=0.001, batch=100; two 4096-d FC layers; embeddings from penultimate 4096-d layer. Protocol: seed 10K, select 5K per iteration for 4 iterations; 10 trials; km = 1.25 kt; őĶ tuned so average cluster size ‚Č• 1.25. Metrics: accuracy (and classification accuracy similarly). Results: Cluster-Margin outperforms baselines on CIFAR10/100; on SVHN it matches Margin and both exceed others; BADGE close to Margin except on SVHN; CoreSet ~Random in these settings. Scalability checks: partitioning minimally impacts BADGE accuracy on CIFAR10/SVHN; Cluster-assignment HAC variant matches full-HAC performance while reducing preprocessing cost.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: - Reduced adaptivity inherent to large-batch active learning; potential redundancy within batches if clustering threshold or km/kt are suboptimal. - HAC preprocessing is O(n^2 log n) and requires embeddings for the entire pool; mitigations (multi-round HAC, cluster-assignment) are approximate and require parameter tuning (őĶ, őĶc). - Dependence on embedding quality from a pretrained/finetuned model; distribution shift or poor embeddings can degrade clustering and diversity. - Theoretical guarantees are provided for a related algorithm (Cluster-MarginV) under strong assumptions (realizable setting, isotropic log-concave distributions, simple hypothesis classes); only approximate equivalence to Cluster-Margin in special cases. - Implementation requires substantial compute (e.g., TPUs) and infrastructure; results may depend on exact training schedules and pretraining on validation split. - Multi-label adaptation samples image‚Äďclass pairs uniformly from selected images, which may not optimally target informative classes.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: - Extend theory to more realistic settings: noisy labels, agnostic learning, non-linear hypotheses, general distributions, and direct bounds for Cluster-Margin. - Adaptive or learned clustering thresholds and dynamic reclustering as the representation evolves; incremental/online HAC or alternative scalable diversity mechanisms (e.g., approximate nearest neighbors, submodular optimization). - Improved multi-label acquisition strategies that jointly select image‚Äďclass pairs using class-conditional uncertainty and inter-class diversity. - Integration with Bayesian uncertainty estimates, ensembling, or calibrated confidence to refine margin-based selection. - Explore self-supervised or foundation-model embeddings and cross-modal tasks; apply to NLP, speech, and structured prediction. - Cost-sensitive and fairness-aware batch active learning; robustness to distribution shift and adversarial examples. - Systems advances: distributed/approximate HAC at web scale, streaming data support, and efficient retraining schedules to balance adaptivity vs. scalability.

research_study_list.title: Improved Algorithms for Agnostic Pool-based Active Classification
research_study_list.abstract:
We consider active learning for binary classification in the agnostic
pool-based setting. The vast majority of works in active learning in the
agnostic setting are inspired by the CAL algorithm where each query is
uniformly sampled from the disagreement region of the current version space.
The sample complexity of such algorithms is described by a quantity known as
the disagreement coefficient which captures both the geometry of the hypothesis
space as well as the underlying probability space. To date, the disagreement
coefficient has been justified by minimax lower bounds only, leaving the door
open for superior instance dependent sample complexities. In this work we
propose an algorithm that, in contrast to uniform sampling over the
disagreement region, solves an experimental design problem to determine a
distribution over examples from which to request labels. We show that the new
approach achieves sample complexity bounds that are never worse than the best
disagreement coefficient-based bounds, but in specific cases can be
dramatically smaller. From a practical perspective, the proposed algorithm
requires no hyperparameters to tune (e.g., to control the aggressiveness of
sampling), and is computationally efficient by means of assuming access to an
empirical risk minimization oracle (without any constraints). Empirically, we
demonstrate that our algorithm is superior to state of the art agnostic active
learning algorithms on image classification datasets.
research_study_list.full_text: Improved Algorithms for Agnostic Pool-based Active ClassiÔ¨Ācation Julian Katz-Samuels 1 Jifan Zhang 1 Lalit Jain 1 Kevin Jamieson1 Abstract We consider active learning for binary classiÔ¨Ā- cation in the agnostic pool-based setting. The vast majority of works in active learning in the agnostic setting are inspired by the CAL algo- rithm where each query is uniformly sampled from the disagreement region of the current ver- sion space. The sample complexity of such algo- rithms is described by a quantity known as the disagreement coefÔ¨Ācient which captures both the geometry of the hypothesis space as well as the underlying probability space. To date, the dis- agreement coefÔ¨Ācient has been justiÔ¨Āed by min- imax lower bounds only, leaving the door open for superior instance dependent sample complex- ities. In this work we propose an algorithm that, in contrast to uniform sampling over the disagree- ment region, solves an experimental design prob- lem to determine a distribution over examples from which to request labels. We show that the new approach achieves sample complexity bounds that are never worse than the best disagreement coefÔ¨Ācient-based bounds, but in speciÔ¨Āc cases can be dramatically smaller. From a practical perspective, the proposed algorithm requires no hyperparameters to tune (e.g., to control the ag- gressiveness of sampling), and is computation- ally efÔ¨Ācient by means of assuming access to an empirical risk minimization oracle (without any constraints). Empirically, we demonstrate that our algorithm is superior to state of the art agnostic active learning algorithms on image classiÔ¨Ācation datasets. 1. Introduction Most applications of machine learning have an enormous amount of unlabeled data. Yet, many powerful machine learning methods require that this data be labeled and reli- 1Paul G. Allen School of Computer Science and Engineering, University of Washington, Seattle, W A. Correspondence to: Kevin Jamieson <jamieson@cs.washington.edu>. able labels are costly since they require human intervention. The cost of providing labels has become one of the main bottlenecks in applications of machine learning, generating much interest in the problem of active classiÔ¨Ācation where the learner is given an unlabeled pool of examples and her goal is to identify an accurate hypothesis using the minimum number of labels possible (Settles, 2011). One of the most popular algorithmic paradigms is disagreement-based active classiÔ¨Ācation (Hanneke et al., 2014). Under this approach, after observing k labels a version space Vk of the most promising classiÔ¨Āers is main- tained, and the learner queries an example x if there are two hypotheses h1 and h2 belonging to Vk that disagree on the label of x. This approach has received much attention because it applies to generic hypothesis classes, it can be made robust to label noise, and it can be efÔ¨Ācient by using a constrained cost-sensitive classiÔ¨Ācation oracle, a problem for which there are many reasonable heuristics (Agarwal et al., 2018; Beygelzimer et al., 2010). However, disagreement-based active classiÔ¨Ācation suffers from two signiÔ¨Ācant shortcomings. First, it queries uni- formly any example on which there is disagreement even though intuitively some of these examples may be much more informative than others. Second, disagreement-based active classiÔ¨Ācation algorithms tend to take a naive union bound over all hypotheses, which ignores many of the de- pendencies among the hypotheses. Indeed, recent work in pure exploration combinatorial and linear bandits has shown that such naive union bounds can be highly subopti- mal and have a signiÔ¨Ācant impact on empirical performance (Cao & Krishnamurthy, 2019; Jain & Jamieson, 2019; Katz- Samuels et al., 2020). Given that these naive union bounds are very loose and appear in the conÔ¨Ādence bounds used by the algorithms, in practice, many works instead replace these union bounds with a constant that can be tuned to control the aggressiveness of the algorithm (Beygelzimer et al., 2010; Huang et al., 2015). Unfortunately, this constant introduces a hyperparameter to the active learning algorithm that is difÔ¨Ācult to set before seeing lots of data. We design a new algorithm for pool-based active classi- Ô¨Ācation that addresses these shortcomings. It optimizes a novel experimental design objective that Ô¨Ānds the best subset of examples in the disagreement region to query in arXiv:2105.06499v1  [cs.LG]  13 May 2021Improved Algorithms for Agnostic Pool-based Active ClassiÔ¨Ācation order to identify the best classiÔ¨Āer. It avoids wasteful union bounds by adapting to the geometry of the hypothesis space and thus avoiding the need to choose hyperparameters. We introduce a new notion of sample complexity inspired by experimental design that improves on disagreement-based active classiÔ¨Ācation by a factor up to ‚ąönwhere nis the size of the pool while being only a logarithmic factor worse than disagreement-based learning in the worst case. 1.1. Preliminaries Let Xdenote the input space, and let {x1,...,x n}‚äāX denote a pool of examples. Let Hdenote a class of hypothe- ses where each h : X ‚Ü¶‚Üí{0,1}assigns a label to each example in the pool. Let Hx := {(h(xi))i‚ąą[n] : h ‚ąąH} denote the set of labelings over the pool induced by the hypothesis class H. Let ddenote the VC dimension of H. When example i‚ąą[n] is queried, the agent receives label Yi ‚ąľBern(ő∑i) where ő∑= (ő∑i)n i=1 ‚ąą[0,1]n. We deÔ¨Āne the error of a hypothesis h ‚ąąH on the pool of examples as given by err(h) = 1 n n‚ąĎ i=1 P(Yi Őł= h(xi)) (1) = 1 n ‚ąĎ i‚ąą[n] ő∑i(1 ‚ąíh(xi)) + (1‚ąíő∑i)h(xi). Let h‚ąó := arg minh‚ąąHerr(h) be the hypothesis of mini- mum error, and let őĹ = err(h‚ąó). The goal in active classi- Ô¨Ācation is to Ô¨Ānd an h ‚ąąH with error close to that of h‚ąó using as few label queries as possible. In this paper, we quantify performance as follows: Problem. Agnostic Pool Based PAC Active ClassiÔ¨Āca- tion: Given ŌĶ> 0,őī ‚ąą(0,1), identify an ŌĶ-good classiÔ¨Āer, that is, an h ‚ąą Hsuch that err(h) ‚ąíerr(h‚ąó) ‚Č§ ŌĶ with probability at least 1 ‚ąíőīusing as few labels as possible. Remark 1. The goal of Ô¨Ānding an ŌĶ-good classiÔ¨Āer over a pool of examples is closely related to the goal of using an active classiÔ¨Ācation algorithm to Ô¨Ānd a classiÔ¨Āer with good generalization. Suppose VCdim(H) = dand let D be a distribution over X√ó{ 0,1}. For i = 1 ,...,n let (xi,yi) ‚ąľD. If ňÜhsatisÔ¨Āes err(ňÜh) ‚Č§minh‚ąąHerr(h) + ŌĶ, then with probability at least 1 ‚ąíőī P(x,y)‚ąľD(ňÜh(x) Őł= y) ‚Č§ min h‚ąąH P(x,y)‚ąľD(h(x) Őł= y) + ŌĶ+ O (‚ąö dln(1/őī) n ) . by standard passive generalization bounds (Boucheron et al., 2005). 1.2. Main contributions We brieÔ¨āy summarize our contributions: ‚ÄĘ We cast pool-based active binary classiÔ¨Ācation as anadap- tive experimental design problem that computes an op- timal sampling distribution over the pool of unlabelled examples. We demonstrate that an ŌĶ-good classiÔ¨Āer can be obtained with probability at least 1 ‚ąíőīby requesting just ő≥‚ąó(ŌĶ) +ŌĀ‚ąó(ŌĶ) log(1/őī) labels if examples to label are drawn from the optimal design, whereő≥‚ąó(ŌĶ) and ŌĀ‚ąó(ŌĶ) are problem-dependent quantities deÔ¨Āned in the next section. ‚ÄĘ Since this optimal design uses problem dependent infor- mation like ő∑, it is not a constructive strategy or algorithm for a learner. Treating the sample complexity achieved by this optimal design as a target, we design an algorithm that performs sequential stages of experimental design to match the sample complexity of the optimal design, ő≥‚ąó(ŌĶ) + ŌĀ‚ąó(ŌĶ) log(1/őī) up to a log(1/ŌĶ) factor. The algo- rithm employs the use of a novel estimator that appeals to a chaining argument. Unfortunately, the method is not computationally efÔ¨Ācient. ‚ÄĘ We propose a second algorithm that is computationally efÔ¨Ācient given access to an empirical risk minimization or- acle. The price for computational tractability is a slightly worse sample complexity. Besides being computationally efÔ¨Ācient, our approach avoids the need to tune hyper- parameters and the use of a constrained empirical risk minimization oracle which are required by other active learning algorithms (Beygelzimer et al., 2010; Huang et al., 2015). ‚ÄĘ We compare our sample complexity results to those of state-of-the-art disagreement-based learning algorithms that are given in terms of the so-called disagreement coef- Ô¨Ācient. We demonstrate that our results, up to log factors, are never worse than previous results, but can be substan- tially better in certain cases. ‚ÄĘ Empirically, we compare our procedure to state-of-the- art algorithms for the agnostic setting including vari- ants of the importance weighted active learning algo- rithm (IW AL) (Beygelzimer et al., 2010) and active cover (Huang et al., 2015). We demonstrate that our method is superior across four image classiÔ¨Ācation tasks. 2. Experimental Design for Active ClassiÔ¨Ācation We seek to identify an ŌĶ-good classiÔ¨Āer by seeing as few labels as possible. To this end, we can take motivation from experimental design to consider the optimal sampling distribution over our pool of unlabeled examples [n]. For an arbitrary distribution őĽ ‚ąą‚Ė≥n := {p ‚ąąRn : pi ‚Č•0,‚ąÄi ‚ąą [n]; ‚ąĎn i=1 pi = 1}suppose we sampled I1,¬∑¬∑¬∑ ,It ‚ąľőĽand then observed ys for each s‚ąą[t]. Then an unbiased natural estimator for the error of a classiÔ¨Āer h‚ąąH deÔ¨Āned by (1)Improved Algorithms for Agnostic Pool-based Active ClassiÔ¨Ācation is given by ňúerr(h) = 1 t t‚ąĎ s=1 1/n őĽIs 1 {h(xIs) Őł= ys}. Indeed, by i.i.d. sampling from őĽ, we have for any s‚ąą[t] E[ňúerr(h))] = E [1/n őĽIs 1 {h(xIs) Őł= ys} ] = n‚ąĎ i=1 P(Is = i)1/n őĽi E[1 {h(xi) Őł= ys}|Is = i] = 1 n n‚ąĎ i=1 P(Yi Őł= h(xi)) = err(h) since by deÔ¨Ānition, P(Is = i) = őĽi. Likewise, an estimator for the excess risk is given by ňúerr(h) ‚ąíňúerr(h‚ąó) = (2) 1 t t‚ąĎ s=1 1/n őĽi (1 {h(xIs) Őł= ys}‚ąí1 {h‚ąó(xIs) Őł= ys}). It is straightforward to show that the variance of ňúerr(h) ‚ąí ňúerr(h‚ąó) is upper bounded by 1 n ‚ąĎn i=1 1 őĽin2 1 {h‚ąó(xi) Őł= h(xi)}, using the upper bound 1 {h(xI) Őł= ys} ‚ąí 1 {h‚ąó(xI) Őł= ys}) ‚Č§1 {h‚ąó(xI) Őł= h(xI)}. Applying Bern- stein‚Äôs inequality (and ignoring the1/tterm) with probabil- ity at least 1 ‚ąíőī |ňúerr(h) ‚ąíňúerr(h‚ąó) ‚ąí(err(h) ‚ąíerr(h‚ąó))|‚™Ö ‚ąö‚ąĎn i=1 1 őĽin2 1 {h‚ąó(xi) Őł= h(xi)}log(|Hx|/őī) t . (3) This then suggests that to estimate the excess error of this particular h with probability at least 1 ‚ąíőī, it suf- Ô¨Āces to take t large enough to make the RHS of (3) less than ŌĶ. To upper bound the excess risk of ev- ery h ‚ąą Hsimultaneously, it sufÔ¨Āces to take t ‚Č• suph‚ąąH ‚ąĎn i=1 1 őĽin2 1 {h‚ąó(xi)Őł=h(xi)} max{ŌĶ2,(err(h)‚ąíerr(h‚ąó))2} log(|H|/őī). If we seek to minimize the total number of observations, we simply minimize over all őĽ‚ąą‚Ė≥n, motivating the complexity mea- sure: ŌĀ‚ąó(ŌĶ) := inf őĽ‚ąą‚Ė≥n sup h‚ąąH\{h‚ąó} ‚ąĎn i=1 1 őĽin2 1 {h‚ąó(xi) Őł= h(xi)} max(err(h) ‚ąíerr(h‚ąó),ŌĶ)2 . Thus, we‚Äôd expect that if t ‚Č•ŌĀ‚ąó(ŌĶ) log(|H|/őī) samples are drawn from the őĽ that minimizes ŌĀ‚ąó(ŌĶ), then ňÜh = arg minh‚ąąHňúerr(h) will be ŌĶ-good. 2.1. Sidestepping the Naive Union Bound A signiÔ¨Ācant shortcoming of the standard approach of ap- plying Bernstein‚Äôs inequality with a naive union bound is that the the naive union bound incurs an additional factor of log(|Hx |) in the sample complexity. For inÔ¨Ānite classes, log(|Hx |) can be replaced by the VC-dimension of Hx, however this can still be very loose. In practice, active learn- ing algorithms replace log(|Hx |) by a tunable parameter C0 (Beygelzimer et al., 2010; Huang et al., 2015). Ideally C0 would be chosen via cross-validation but since our data is being chosen adaptively, under an active algorithm that depends on C0, it is unclear how to make the choice a priori. To improve upon the naive union-bound we appeal to re- sults from empirical process theory. Appealing to the Talagrand/Bousquet inequality (Boucheron et al., 2005), for all h ‚ąą H, especially the empirical risk minimizer ňÜh= arg minh‚ąąHňúerr(h), we have ňúerr(h) ‚ąíňúerr(h‚ąó) ‚ąí(err(h) ‚ąíerr h‚ąó) ‚Č§2E[sup h‚ąąH |ňúerr(h) ‚ąíňúerr(h‚ąó) ‚ąí(err(h) ‚ąíerr h‚ąó)|] + ‚ąö suph‚ąąH ‚ąĎn i=1 1 őĽin2 1 {h‚ąó(xi) Őł= h(xi)}log(1/őī) t + 4 supi‚ąą[n] 1/őĽilog(1/őī) 3t . Traditionally, we compute the expectation of the suprema using symmeterization to obtain the Rademacher complex- ity of H\{h‚ąó}. In general, the Rademacher complexity is within a log(n) factor of the Gaussian Width (Bartlett & Mendelson, 2002). Discussion of Theorem 1 Theorem 1 above demonstrates the existence of an estimator that avoids any dependence on log(|Hx |). The construc- tion of the estimator in Theorem 1 uses generic chaining, a technique that builds a highly optimized union bound to avoid extraneous logarithmic factors (Talagrand, 2014). Generic chaining is most easily applied when a given esti- mator ňÜő∑satisÔ¨Āes the property that ňúerr(h,ňÜő∑) ‚ąíňúerr(h‚Ä≤,ňÜő∑) is sub-Gaussian for every ‚Äúdirection‚ÄĚh‚ąíh‚Ä≤of interest (e.g., see (Katz-Samuels et al., 2020)). Though the ňÜő∑(Importance) ő≥ estimator has sub-Gamma tails in general, ruling out its use, the following result shows that for h‚ąíh‚Ä≤ in a ball under a certain norm, we can construct an estimator for ňúerr(h,ňÜő∑) ‚ąíňúerr(h‚Ä≤,ňÜő∑) with a sub-Gaussian-like tail. Proposition 4. Fix őĽ ‚ąą‚Ė≥n, őī ‚ąą(0,1), and h,h‚Ä≤ ‚ąąH. If T samples are taken from őĽ and ňÜő∑ := ňÜő∑(Importance) ő≥ is computed with ő≥ = ‚ąö log(2/őī) 3 ‚ąĎn i=1 1 őĽin2 1 {h(xi)Őł=h‚Ä≤(xi)}then with probability at least 1 ‚ąíőī |[ňúerr(h,ňÜő∑) ‚ąíňúerr(h‚Ä≤,ňÜő∑)] ‚ąí[err(h) ‚ąíerr(h‚Ä≤)]| (‚ąö 2 3 + 1 )‚ąö 2 ‚ąĎn i=1 1 őĽin2 1 {h(xi) Őł= h‚Ä≤(xi)}log(2 őī) t . The idea behind Theorem 1 is to apply generic chaining to all h‚ąíh‚Ä≤, but to use a different ňÜő∑(speciÔ¨Ācally, a different ő≥) based on the size of h‚ąíh‚Ä≤prescribed by Proposition 4. Details of the technique can be found in the supplementary materials. 4.2. Computationally EfÔ¨Ācient Experimental Design In this section, we discuss how to solve (5) efÔ¨Āciently given access to a weighted empirical risk minimiza- tion oracle, which we will introduce shortly. First, note that minimizing (5) is equivalent to minimizing Eő∂‚ąľN(0,I)[maxh‚ąąHf(őĽ; h; ő∂)] with respect to őĽwhere f(őĽ; h; ő∂) := ‚ąĎ i‚ąą[n](ňúhk(xi)‚ąíh(xi)) ő∂i nőĽ1/2 i 2‚ąík+1+ ňúerr(h,ňÜő∑k‚ąí1)‚ąíňúerr(ňúhk,ňÜő∑k‚ąí1) := ‚ąĎ i‚ąą[n](ňúhk(xi)‚ąíh(xi)) ő∂i nőĽ1/2 i 2‚ąík+1+‚ąĎ i‚ąą[n](1‚ąí2ňÜő∑k‚ąí1,i)(ňúhk(xi)‚ąíh(xi)) . It is known that Eő∂‚ąľN(0,I)[maxh‚ąąHf(őĽ; h; ő∂)] is convex in őĽ(Katz-Samuels et al., 2020), hence we perform the mini- mization over őĽvia stochastic mirror descent with stochas- tic gradient g(őĽ,ő∂) = ‚ąáf(őĽ,ňúh; ő∂) where ő∂ ‚ąľN(0,I) and ňúh‚ąąarg maxh‚ąąHf(őĽ,h; ő∂). To obtain ňúhfor a Ô¨Āxed őĽand ő∂, Ô¨Ārst note that the value maxh‚ąąHf(őĽ; h; ő∂) is equal to min r‚ąąR+ rsubject to ar+ b+ max h‚ąąH ‚ąĎ i‚ąą[n] (cir+ di)h(xi) ‚Č§0 where a = ‚ąí2‚ąík+1 ‚ąí‚ąĎ i‚ąą[n](1 ‚ąí2ňÜő∑k,i)ňúhk(xi), b =‚ąĎ i‚ąą[n] ő∂ nőĽ1/2 i ňúhk(xi), ci = 1 ‚ąí2ňÜő∑k‚ąí1,i and di = ‚ąí ő∂ nőĽ1/2 i . For any Ô¨Āxed positive value of r it sufÔ¨Āces to check the constraint. We can then use a line search procedure to Ô¨Ānd the minimizing value of r(details in Appendix K). Thus we have reduced to checking the constraint for a Ô¨Āxed r ‚ąąR+. SpeciÔ¨Ācally, the difÔ¨Āculty is to solve for maxh‚ąąH ‚ąĎ i‚ąą[n] wi ¬∑h(xi) where wi are arbitrary weights. This can be reduced to weighted 0/1-loss minimization problem that is solvable by a weighted classiÔ¨Ācation oracle: oracle({ňúwi,ňúxi,ňúyi}n i=1):=arg min h‚ąąH ‚ąĎ i‚ąą[n] ňúwi¬∑1{h(ňúxi) Őł= ňúyi} for inputs {ňúwi,ňúxi,ňúyi}n i=1. Then, max h‚ąąH ‚ąĎ i‚ąą[n] wi ¬∑h(xi) = oracle({|wi|,xi,1{wi ‚Č•0}}n i=1). 5. Implementation and Experiments In the previous section we reduced the experimental design objective of (5) to a weighted 0/1 loss classiÔ¨Ācation problem using weights that are functions of the estimated vector ňÜő∑. In practice we replace this 0/1 loss with a surrogate convex loss, namely the logistic loss. However, to implement Al- gorithm 2 we still have to specify the choice of estimator ňÜő∑. Though the estimator speciÔ¨Āed in Theorem 1 is theoretically grounded, it is difÔ¨Ācult to implement in practice since it involves a costly constrained linear optimization problem over the set of hypothesis in Hk. As described in Remark 2, it is still possible to have a theoretical guarantee for other estimators such as the IPS estimator. As described precisely in Appendix K, in our implementation we take the estimate for ňÜő∑k to be [ ňÜő∑(Naive) k ] i = average({y(j) s : I(j) s = i,s ‚ąą[Nj],j ‚ąą[k]}),Improved Algorithms for Agnostic Pool-based Active ClassiÔ¨Ācation i.e. a simple average of the labels we see. Here I(j) s indexes the s-th query we made in round j. In our experiments we only considered the persistent noise setting (i.e., querying the same image more than once would always return the same label as before, or formally, ő∑i ‚ąą{0,1}). Thus, if we sample a point x(j) Is (i.e., I(j) s ) more than once, we set y(j) s to be the previously observed label and we did not count this observation in our count of total labels taken. To take advantage of all of the labels observed so far, we also employ a water-Ô¨Ālling technique for sampling in practice (details in Appendix K). Baselines. To validate Algorithm 2 we conducted a set of experiments against the following baselines that are con- sidered to be state-of-the-art theoretically-justiÔ¨Āed methods in disagreement based active learning. Our set of methods are chosen based on the ones considered in (Huang et al., 2015), the most recent work of relevance. Details on the precise implementations of these methods are available in the supplementary materials in Appendix K. ‚ÄĘ Passive: We considered a passive baseline where we uni- formly at random choose samples from our pool, retrain our model on our current samples and report the accuracy. ‚ÄĘ Importance Weighted Active Learning (IWAL) : IWAL was originally introduced in Beygelzimer et al. (2009) and is an active learning algorithm in the streaming set- ting. Our implementation is based on the algorithm pre- sented in Beygelzimer et al. (2010) which we refer to as IW AL0. We also consider variants, IW AL1, and oracular versions ORA-IWAL0, ORA-IWAL1 detailed in Huang et al. (2015). ‚ÄĘ Online Active Cover (OAC): OAC is described in Huang et al. (2015). We used the implementation of OAC that is available in V owpal Wabbit (Agarwal et al., 2014). Datasets. We evaluate on the following four real datasets. ‚ÄĘ MNIST 0-4 vs 5-9 (LeCun et al., 1998). We considered the standard MNIST dataset but in a binary setting where digits 0-4 are labelled as 0, and 5-9 are labelled as 1. Our pool has 50000 images in total, and we classiÔ¨Āed based on the Ô¨āattened images (784 dimensions). ‚ÄĘ SVHN 2 vs 7 (Netzer et al., 2011). We considered the binary classiÔ¨Ācation problem of determining whether a digit was a 2 or a 7 (ignoring all other images). To prevent the logistic classiÔ¨Āer from overÔ¨Ātting to arbitrary labels and to restrict the hypothesis class H, we downsample the images to 512 dimensional feature vectors through PCA. There are 16180 images in total. ‚ÄĘ CIFAR Bird vs Plane (Netzer et al., 2011). We con- sidered the binary classiÔ¨Ācation problem of determining whether a digit was a bird or a plane (ignoring all other images). To prevent the logistic classiÔ¨Āer from overÔ¨Ātting to arbitrary labels and to restrict the hypothesis class H, we downsample the images to 576 dimensional feature vectors through PCA. There are 10000 images in total. ‚ÄĘ FashionMNIST T-shirt vs Pants(Xiao et al., 2017). We considered the binary classiÔ¨Ācation problem of T-shirt vs Pants. Our pool has 12000 images in total, and we classiÔ¨Āed based on the Ô¨āattened images (784 dimensions). Implementations. We use two implementations to measure the performances of the algorithms. ‚ÄĘ Implementation from V owpal Wabbit (Agarwal et al., 2014) that is used by Huang et al. (2015). The imple- mentation employs an online learner that only updates based on the latest queried label, therefore has time com- plexity that scales linearly in the number of images n. ‚ÄĘ For our implementation in a batched setting, we retrain the entire classiÔ¨Āer to convergence every time new labels become available. We Ô¨Ānd that the online learner of above can perform signiÔ¨Ācantly better than our batched learner during the Ô¨Ārst few batches of training. However, our implementation has more stable accuracies during the course of training and performs slightly superior (<1%) in Ô¨Ānal accuracy. This comes at a cost of an O(n2) time complexity, which is too expensive in some of the settings. In particular, we only use the V owpal Wabbit implementa- tion for the OAC experiments and the oracular variants of IWAL algorithms for our MNIST experiement, due to the high computation cost for running these algorithms with exhaustive hyperparameter search. However, we think this is still a fair comparison when evaluating some baselines using the the two implementations since it is the best one can achieve for those baselines within a computation budget (single machine with state-of-art commercialized CPU that runs for a month). Hypothesis Class. In our implementation, we took the hypothesis space to be the set of linear separators in the underlying feature space. We used the logistic regression implementation in Scikit-learn (Pedregosa et al., 2011) for our underlying classiÔ¨Ācation oracle. Figure 1.MNIST Performance  Figure 2.SVHN PerformanceImproved Algorithms for Agnostic Pool-based Active ClassiÔ¨Ācation Figure 3.CIFAR Performance  Figure 4.FashionMNIST Discussion. For each of the binary classiÔ¨Ācation datasets, we plot the running maximum accuracy on the unlabelled pool against the number of queries taken as in Figure 1,2,4,3 (full scale images included in Appendix L). The passive curves are evaluated based on the averages of 10 runs. In the CIFAR experiment, ACED is an average over5 runs. We Ô¨Ānd the curve in this setting to be very consistent, and that the standard deviations are minimal for visualization. All of the other curves are evaluated based on a single run. For baselines algorithms proposed in the streaming setting (vari- ants of IW AL and OAC), in each round we uniformly sample an example from the pool, and feed a Ô¨Āxed number of passes. We select the best C0 based on which hyperparameter set- ting takes the least amount of queries to reach the same level of accuracy. Detailed hyperparameters considered for the baselines are included in Appendix M. Furthermore, to demonstrate active gains in generalization, we include plots on holdout test sets in Appendix N. On all four datasets, our algorithm outperforms other base- lines by taking much fewer queries to reach the passive accuracy on the entire dataset. Sometimes the active learn- ing algorithms even beat the passive accuracy on the whole dataset, which is a known phenomenon of active learning studied by Mussmann & Liang (2018). For the MNIST dataset, we do not include performance curves for the orac- ular variants of IW AL, since the V owpal Wabbit implemen- tation turns out to be performing at random chance. We also notice that OAC stops taking queries very early on (no longer making queries when given more passes over the pool). However, when increasing C0, the aggressiveness to make a query, OAC starts performing worst than pas- sive pretty easily. We include Figure 9 in the appendix to demonstrate how sensitive the OAC curves are to the hyper- parameter C0, which one cannot tune in real applications. As a special case, on the FashionMNIST dataset, our bi- nary classiÔ¨Ācation task is linearly separable and the baseline methods fail miserably. For all of the IWAL algorithms on this dataset, we searched in an extended range of hy- perparameters than the ones used in the other three tasks. When Ô¨Āxing the random order of the stream, however, all of the baselines become equivalent, and perform almost identical to passive. Since in practice, only one set of hy- perparameters can be deployed, this again demonstrates the shortcoming of these baseline algorithms, whereas our method does not rely on any aggressiveness hyperparameter. 6. Related Work and Discussion Active ClassiÔ¨Ācation: Active classiÔ¨Ācation has received much attention with a large number of theoretical and em- pirical works (see (Hanneke et al., 2014) and (Settles, 2011) for excellent surveys). Cohn et al. (1994) initiated research into the study of disagreement based active classiÔ¨Ācation al- gorithms, proposing CAL for the realizable setting. Balcan et al. (2009) extended disagreement-based active classiÔ¨Āca- tion to the agnostic case, introducing the method, A2. Han- neke (2007) provided a general analysis of A2 in terms of the disagreement coefÔ¨Ācient, with follow-up works improv- ing on the sample complexity of this approach (Dasgupta et al., 2007; Hanneke, 2009; Hanneke et al., 2011; Koltchin- skii, 2010; Hanneke et al., 2014). The results in Section 2.2 show that our sample complexities are never worse than the ones obtained by this line of work. An extension of this line of work has aimed to attain simi- lar sample complexities, while leveraging an empirical risk minimization oracle to design more practical algorithms (Dasgupta et al., 2007; Hsu, 2010; Beygelzimer et al., 2010; Huang et al., 2015). With the exception of Huang et al. (2015), these methods tend to have a conservative query policy that samples uniformly in the disagreement region, leading to an onerous label requirement. While Huang et al. (2015) has a more aggressive query policy that does not sample uniformly in the disagreement region, their sam- ple complexity result could also be obtained by sampling uniformly in the disagreement region and, therefore, their theoretical result does not reÔ¨āect gains from a careful se- lection of points in the disagreement region. In particular, the dominant term is still the disagreement coefÔ¨Ācient and, hence, it can be much worse than our sample complexity on instances such as the one in Proposition 3. Recently, Jain & Jamieson (2019) showed that active classi- Ô¨Ācation in the pool-based setting is an instance of combina- torial bandits, an observation that is central to our analysis. They provided the Ô¨Ārst analysis that shows the contribution of each example to the sample complexity providing a more Ô¨Āne-grained result than the disagreement coefÔ¨Ācient. We improve on this work by optimizing the sampling distri- bution in the region of disagreement and using improved estimators such as the one in Theorem 1. Proposition 4 of Katz-Samuels et al. (2020) implies that our sample com- plexity is always better than the sample complexity in Jain & Jamieson (2019). Linear and Combinatorial Bandits. ŌĀ‚ąóhas been shown to be the dominant term in a lower bound for pure exploration linear bandits and combinatorial bandits (Soare et al., 2014; Chen et al., 2017; Fiez et al., 2019). Recently Katz-SamuelsImproved Algorithms for Agnostic Pool-based Active ClassiÔ¨Ācation et al. (2020) introduced the notion of ő≥‚ąó for linear and combinatorial bandits, showing that it is a lower bound for any non-interactive oracle MLE algorithm. One of our contributions is making the connection between the active classiÔ¨Ācation and linear/combinatorial bandit literature, and showing that we can leverage the results from this work to obtain improved sample complexities for agnostic active classiÔ¨Ācation. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Addresses agnostic pool-based binary active classification by replacing uniform sampling over the disagreement region with an adaptive experimental design approach. Introduces new instance-dependent complexity measures (rho*(őĶ), gamma*(őĶ)) that yield label complexity guarantees never worse (up to logarithmic factors) than disagreement-coefficient-based results and can be dramatically better on specific instances. Proposes two algorithms: (1) a fixed-confidence elimination algorithm (ACED) achieving roughly (gamma*(őĶ) + rho*(őĶ) log(1/őī)) log(1/őĶ) labels, and (2) a computationally efficient fixed-budget version using an ERM (weighted classification) oracle with slightly worse complexity. Provides theoretical comparisons against disagreement-based methods and empirical superiority on several image classification tasks without hyperparameter tuning for aggressiveness.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Reformulates pool-based active classification as an adaptive experimental design problem: choose a sampling distribution őĽ over pool points to minimize estimation error of excess risk.
- Defines instance-dependent complexities:
  ‚ÄĘ rho*(őĶ): an optimization over őĽ of a worst-case variance-like term scaling with 1/őĽ_i on points where h and h* disagree, normalized by max(err(h)‚ąíerr(h*), őĶ)^2.
  ‚ÄĘ gamma*(őĶ): a Gaussian-width/Rademacher-complexity-style term capturing expected supremum of a őĽ-weighted Gaussian process over hypothesis differences, again normalized by max(err(h)‚ąíerr(h*), őĶ).
- Avoids loose naive union bounds by using Talagrand/Bousquet inequalities and generic chaining to control empirical process deviations, yielding tighter confidence intervals.
- Algorithms:
  ‚ÄĘ Fixed-confidence ACED (elimination): at stage k, solves a stochastic experimental design program to pick őĽ_k minimizing bounds involving gamma and rho; samples from őĽ_k; forms a specialized estimator (constructed via chaining/Ridge-IPS idea) to compare hypotheses and eliminate suboptimal ones; stops when only one remains.
  ‚ÄĘ Fixed-budget ACED: splits budget into O(log(1/őĶ)) phases; in each, solves a convex design objective approximating the optimal ő≥/ŌĀ trade-off and updates an error-rate estimator; outputs ERM under the final estimator. A computationally efficient variant uses an ERM/weighted classification oracle (implemented via logistic regression), stochastic mirror descent to optimize őĽ, and a water-filling scheme to reuse samples across rounds.
- Theoretical comparisons show rho* and gamma* upper-bound disagreement-based label complexities up to log factors; also provides lower-bound style separations where őł(őĺ)=ő©(‚ąön) but rho*=O(1), gamma*=O(log n).
- Practical implementation details: surrogate logistic loss replaces 0/1 oracle; line-search and mirror-descent compute őĽ; importance-weighted and ridge-IPS inspired estimators; water-filling to align cumulative samples with current őĽ.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Tasks (binary classification on image datasets):
  ‚ÄĘ MNIST: digits 0‚Äď4 vs 5‚Äď9, 50,000 images, 784-d flattened.
  ‚ÄĘ SVHN: 2 vs 7, 16,180 images, PCA to 512 dims.
  ‚ÄĘ CIFAR-10: bird vs plane, 10,000 images, PCA to 576 dims.
  ‚ÄĘ FashionMNIST: T-shirt vs pants, 12,000 images, 784-d flattened.
- Hypothesis class: linear separators; trained via logistic regression (scikit-learn). Experiments assume persistent/noiseless labels (ő∑‚ąą{0,1}).
- Baselines: Passive uniform sampling; IWAL variants (IWAL0, IWAL1, oracle-enhanced ORA-IWAL0/1); Online Active Cover (OAC, Vowpal Wabbit). Baselines‚Äô aggressiveness hyperparameter C0 tuned over grids; streaming-style methods run with specified numbers of passes.
- Implementations: two modes‚ÄĒ(i) online learner via Vowpal Wabbit for OAC and some IWAL variants; (ii) batched retraining logistic regression for ACED and others. ACED optimization via stochastic mirror descent for őĽ, line-search over surrogate objective, water-filling sample reuse.
- Evaluation: plots of running maximum accuracy vs number of queried labels on the pool; additional holdout test set results in appendix. Passive averaged over 10 runs; ACED averaged over 5 runs on CIFAR; others single runs. Findings: ACED consistently reaches passive full-data accuracy with far fewer labels and often surpasses it; OAC sensitive to C0; on FashionMNIST (separable), baselines underperform while ACED excels.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- The fixed-confidence algorithm relies on a sophisticated estimator constructed via generic chaining; it is not computationally efficient in practice.
- The practical fixed-budget algorithm incurs an extra complexity term Ōą*(őĶ) in theory and uses surrogate ERM oracles; theoretical-optimal estimators are replaced with simpler ones (e.g., naive or IPS), creating a gap between theory and practice.
- Optimization requires repeated calls to weighted classification oracles and stochastic mirror descent with Gaussian sampling, which can be computationally heavy; batched retraining has O(n^2) time in some settings.
- Experiments focus on binary linear models and mostly noiseless labels; generalization to multiclass, nonlinear (deep) models, or heavy label noise is not demonstrated.
- New complexity measures (rho*, gamma*, Ōą*) may be hard to compute or approximate in general, limiting direct interpretability or off-the-shelf use.
- Empirical comparisons are limited to a few image tasks and to certain disagreement-based baselines whose streaming nature and hyperparameter sensitivity may disadvantage them in the pool setting.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Develop computationally efficient algorithms attaining the fixed-confidence bounds (reduce reliance on chaining-based estimators) and close the Ōą*(őĶ) gap.
- Practical estimators with strong guarantees under realistic surrogates and without constrained ERM; investigate tighter, data-driven confidence bounds.
- Efficient approximation/estimation of rho*(őĶ) and gamma*(őĶ) for real-world hypothesis classes; design adaptive procedures that implicitly track these complexities.
- Extend to multiclass, multilabel, and structured outputs; integrate with non-linear models (e.g., kernel methods, deep networks) and large-scale pools.
- Robustness to diverse noise models (Tsybakov, adversarial, annotator noise) and domain shift; bridge to streaming active learning.
- Hybrid methods combining disagreement-region intuition with experimental design objectives; analyze active learning with budgeted costs and fairness or other constraints.
- Explore theoretical generalization guarantees linking pool-level őĶ-goodness to out-of-sample performance under modern representation learning.

research_study_list.title: Efficient Active Learning with Abstention
research_study_list.abstract:
The goal of active learning is to achieve the same accuracy achievable by
passive learning, while using much fewer labels. Exponential savings in terms
of label complexity have been proved in very special cases, but fundamental
lower bounds show that such improvements are impossible in general. This
suggests a need to explore alternative goals for active learning. Learning with
abstention is one such alternative. In this setting, the active learning
algorithm may abstain from prediction and incur an error that is marginally
smaller than random guessing. We develop the first computationally efficient
active learning algorithm with abstention. Our algorithm provably achieves
$\mathsf{polylog}(\frac{1}{\varepsilon})$ label complexity, without any low
noise conditions. Such performance guarantee reduces the label complexity by an
exponential factor, relative to passive learning and active learning that is
not allowed to abstain. Furthermore, our algorithm is guaranteed to only
abstain on hard examples (where the true label distribution is close to a fair
coin), a novel property we term \emph{proper abstention} that also leads to a
host of other desirable characteristics (e.g., recovering minimax guarantees in
the standard setting, and avoiding the undesirable ``noise-seeking'' behavior
often seen in active learning). We also provide novel extensions of our
algorithm that achieve \emph{constant} label complexity and deal with model
misspecification.
research_study_list.full_text: EÔ¨Écient Active Learning with Abstention Yinglun Zhu University of Wisconsin-Madison yinglun@cs.wisc.edu Robert Nowak University of Wisconsin-Madison rdnowak@wisc.edu Abstract The goal of active learning is to achieve the same accuracy achievable by passive learning, while using much fewer labels. Exponential savings in terms of label complexity have been proved in very special cases, but fundamental lower bounds show that such improvements are impossible in general. This suggests a need to explore alternative goals for active learning. Learning with abstention is one such alternative. In this setting, the active learning algorithm may abstain from prediction and incur an error that is marginally smaller than random guessing. We develop the Ô¨Ārst computationally eÔ¨Écient active learning algorithm with abstention. Our algorithm provably achievespolylog( 1 őĶ) label complexity, without any low noise conditions. Such performance guarantee reduces the label complexity by an exponential factor, relative to passive learning and active learning that is not allowed to abstain. Furthermore, our algorithm is guaranteed to only abstain on hard examples (where the true label distribution is close to a fair coin), a novel property we termproper abstention that also leads to a host of other desirable characteristics (e.g., recovering minimax guarantees in the standard setting, and avoiding the undesirable ‚Äúnoise-seeking‚ÄĚ behavior often seen in active learning). We also provide novel extensions of our algorithm that achieve constant label complexity and deal with model misspeciÔ¨Ācation. 1 Introduction Active learning aims at learning an accurate classiÔ¨Āer with a small number of labeled data points (Settles, 2009; Hanneke, 2014). Active learning has become increasingly important in modern application of machine learning, where unlabeled data points are abundant yet the labeling process requires expensive time and eÔ¨Äort. Empirical successes of active learning have been observed in many areas (Tong and Koller, 2001; Gal et al., 2017; Sener and Savarese, 2018). In noise-free or certain low-noise cases (i.e., under Massart noise (Massart and N√©d√©lec, 2006)), active learning algorithms withprovable exponential savings over the passive counterpart have been developed (Balcan et al., 2007; Hanneke, 2007; Dasgupta et al., 2009; Hsu, 2010; Dekel et al., 2012; Hanneke, 2014; Zhang and Chaudhuri, 2014; Krishnamurthy et al., 2019; Katz-Samuels et al., 2021). On the other hand, however, not much can be said in the general case. In fact, K√§√§ri√§inen (2006) provides a‚Ą¶( 1 őĶ2 ) lower bound by reducing active learning to a simple mean estimation problem: It takes ‚Ą¶( 1 őĶ2 ) samples to distinguishő∑(x) = 1 2 + őĶ and ő∑(x) = 1 2 ‚ąíőĶ. Even with the relatively benign Tsybakov noise (Tsybakov, 2004), Castro and Nowak (2006, 2008) derive a‚Ą¶(poly(1 őĶ)) lower bound, again, indicating that exponential speedup over passive learning is not possible in general. These fundamental lower bounds lay out statistical barriers to active learning, and suggests considering a reÔ¨Ānement of the label complexity goals in active learning (K√§√§ri√§inen, 2006). Inspecting these lower bounds, one can see that active learning suÔ¨Äers from classifying hard examples that are close to the decision boundary. However,do we really require a trained classiÔ¨Āer to do well on those hard examples? In high-risk domains such as medical imaging, it makes more sense for the classiÔ¨Āer to abstain from making the decision and leave the problem to a human expert. Such idea is formalized under Chow‚Äôs error (Chow, 1970): Whenever the classiÔ¨Āer chooses to abstain, a loss that is barely smaller than random guessing, i.e., 1 2 ‚ąíő≥, is incurred. The parameterő≥ should be thought as a small positive quantity, e.g.,ő≥ = 0.01. The inclusion of abstention is not only practically interesting, but also provides a statistical reÔ¨Ānement of the label complexity goal of active learning: Achieving exponential improvement under Chow‚Äôs excess error. When abstention is allowed as an action, Puchkin and Zhivotovskiy (2021) shows, for the Ô¨Ārst time, that exponential 1 arXiv:2204.00043v2  [stat.ML]  15 Oct 2022improvement in label complexity can be achieved by active learning in the general setting. However, the approach provided in Puchkin and Zhivotovskiy (2021) can not be eÔ¨Éciently implemented. Their algorithm follows the disagreement-based approach and requires maintaining a version space and checking whether or not an example lies in the region of disagreement. It is not clear how to generally implement these operations besides enumeration (Beygelzimer et al., 2010). Moreover, their algorithm relies on an Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) oracle, which is known to be NP-Hard even for a simple linear hypothesis class (Guruswami and Raghavendra, 2009). In this paper, we break the computational barrier and design an eÔ¨Écient active learning algorithm with exponential improvement in label complexity relative to conventional passive learning. The algorithm relies on weighted square loss regression oracle, which can be eÔ¨Éciently implemented in many cases (Krishnamurthy et al., 2017, 2019; Foster et al., 2018, 2020). The algorithm also abstains properly, i.e., abstain only when it is the optimal choice, which allows us to easily translate the guarantees to thestandard excess error. Along the way, we propose new noise-seeking noise conditions and show that: ‚Äúuncertainty-based‚ÄĚ active learners can be easily trapped, yet our algorithm provably overcome these noise-seeking conditions. As an extension, we also provide the Ô¨Ārst algorithm that enjoysconstant label complexity for ageneral set of regression functions. 1.1 Problem setting Let Xdenote the input space andYdenote the label space. We focus on the binary classiÔ¨Ācation problem where Y= {+1,‚ąí1}. The joint distribution overX√óY is denoted asDXY. We use DX to denote the marginal distribution over the input spaceX, and useDY|x to denote the conditional distribution ofYwith respect to anyx‚ąąX. We deÔ¨Āneő∑(x) := Py‚ąľDY|x(y= +1) as the conditional probability of taking a positive label. We consider the standard active learning setup where(x,y) ‚ąľDXY but y is observed only after a label querying. We consider hypothesis classH: X‚ÜíY . For any classiÔ¨Āerh‚ąąH, its (standard) error is deÔ¨Āned as err(h) := P(x,y)‚ąľDXY(h(x) Őł= y). Function approximation. We focus on the case where the hypothesis classHis induced from a set of regression functionsF: X ‚Üí[0,1] that predicts the conditional probabilityő∑(x). We writeH= HF := {hf : f ‚ąąF} where hf(x) := sign(2f(x) ‚ąí1). The ‚Äúsize‚ÄĚ ofFis measured by the well-known complexity measure: the Pseudo dimension Pdim(F) (Pollard, 1984; Haussler, 1989, 1995). We assumePdim(F) <‚ąě throughout the paper.1 Following existing works in active learning (Dekel et al., 2012; Krishnamurthy et al., 2017, 2019) and contextual bandits (Agarwal et al., 2012; Foster et al., 2018; Foster and Rakhlin, 2020; Simchi-Levi and Xu, 2020), we make the followingrealizability assumption. Assumption 1(Realizability). The learner is given a set of regressorsF: X‚Üí [0,1] such that there exists a f‚čÜ ‚ąąF characterize the true conditional probability, i.e.,f‚čÜ = ő∑. The realizability assumption allowsrich function approximation, which strictly generalizes the setting with linear function approximation studied in active learning (e.g., in (Dekel et al., 2012)). We relax Assumption 1 in Section 4.2 to deal with model misspeciÔ¨Ācation. Regression oracle. We consider a regression oracle overF, which is extensively studied in the literature in active learning and contextual bandits (Krishnamurthy et al., 2017, 2019; Foster et al., 2018, 2020). Given any setSof weighted examples(w,x,y ) ‚ąąR+ √óX√óY as input, the regression oracle outputs ňÜf = arg min f‚ąąF ‚ąĎ (w,x,y)‚ąąS w(f(x) ‚ąíy)2. (1) The regression oracle solves a convex optimization problem with respect to the regression function, and admits closed-form solutions in many cases, e.g., it is reduced to least squares whenf is linear. Beyond disagreement-based agnostic active learning.Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 27, 2014. 12A Additional related work Learning with Chow‚Äôs excess error is closely related to learning under Massart noise (Massart and N√©d√©lec, 2006), which assumes that no data point has conditional expectation close to the decision boundary, i.e., P(|ő∑(x) ‚ąí1/2|‚Č§ ŌĄ0) = 0 for some constantŌĄ0 >0. Learning with Massart noise is commonly studied in active learning (Balcan et al., 2007; Hanneke, 2014; Zhang and Chaudhuri, 2014; Krishnamurthy et al., 2019), where ňúO(ŌĄ‚ąí2 0 ) type of guarantees are achieved. Instead of making explicit assumptions on the underlying distribution, learning with Chow‚Äôs excess error empowers the learner with the ability to abstain: There is no need to make predictions on hard data points that are close to the decision boundary, i.e.,{x: |ő∑(x) ‚ąí1/2|‚Č§ ő≥}. Learning with Chow‚Äôs excess error thus works on more general settings and still enjoys theňúO(ő≥‚ąí2) type of guarantee as learning under Massart noise (Puchkin and Zhivotovskiy, 2021).7 We show in Section 3 that statistical guarantees achieved under Chow‚Äôs excess error can be directly translated to guarantees under (usual and more challenging versions of) Massart/Tsybakov noise (Massart and N√©d√©lec, 2006; Tsybakov, 2004). Active learning at aim competing the best in-class classiÔ¨Āer with few labels. A long line of work directly works with the set of classiÔ¨Āers (Balcan et al., 2007; Hanneke, 2007, 2014; Huang et al., 2015; Puchkin and Zhivotovskiy, 2021), where the algorithms are developed with (in general) hard-to-implement ERM oracles (Guruswami and Raghavendra, 2009) and the the guarantees dependence on the so-called disagreement coeÔ¨Écient (Hanneke, 2014). More recently, learning with function approximation have been studied inactive learning and contextual bandits (Dekel et al., 2012; Agarwal et al., 2012; Foster et al., 2018; Krishnamurthy et al., 2019). The function approximation scheme permits eÔ¨Écient regression oracles, which solve convex optimization problems with respect to regression functions (Krishnamurthy et al., 2017, 2019; Foster et al., 2018). It can also be analyzed with the scale-sensitive version of disagreement coeÔ¨Écient, which is usually tighter than the original one (Foster et al., 2020; Russo and Van Roy, 2013). Our algorithms are inspired Krishnamurthy et al. (2019), where the authors study active learning under the standard excess error. The main deviation from Krishnamurthy et al. (2019) is that we need tomanually construct a classiÔ¨ĀerňÜhwith an abstention option andňÜh /‚ąąH, which leads to diÔ¨Äerences in the analysis of excess error and label complexity. We borrow techniques developed in contextual bandits Russo and Van Roy (2013); Foster et al. (2020) to analyze our algorithm. Although one can also apply our algorithms in the nonparametric regime with proper pre-processing schemes such discretizations, our algorithm primarily works in the parametric setting with Ô¨Ānite pseudo dimension (Haussler, 1995) and Ô¨Ānite (value function) disagreement coeÔ¨Écient (Foster et al., 2020). Active learning has also been studied in the nonparametric regime (Castro and Nowak, 2008; Koltchinskii, 2010; Minsker, 2012; Locatelli et al., 2017). Notably, Shekhar et al. (2021) studies Chow‚Äôs excess error with margin-type of assumptions. Their setting is diÔ¨Äerent to ours andpoly(1 őĶ) label complexities are achieved. If abundant amounts of data points are allowed to be exactly at the decision boundary, i.e.,ő∑(x) = 1 2 , Kpotufe et al. (2021) recently shows that, in the nonparametric regime, no active learner can outperform the passive counterpart. B Why Chow‚Äôs excess error helps? G Omitted details for Section 4.1 We introduce a new perspective for designing and analyzing active learning algorithms in Appendix G.1 (with new notations introduced). Based on this new perspective, we present our algorithm and its theoretical guarantees in Appendix G.2. Supporting lemmas are deferred to Appendix G.3. G.1 The perspective: Regret minimization with selective sampling We view active learning as a decision making problem: at each round, the learner selects an action, suÔ¨Äers a loss (that may not be observable), and decides to query the label or not. At a high level, the learner aims at simultaneously minimizing the regret and the number of queries; and will randomly return a classiÔ¨Āer/decision rule at the end of the learning process. The perspective is inspired by the seminal results derived in Dekel et al. (2012), where the authors study active learning with linear regression functions and focus on standard excess error guarantees. With this regret minimization perspective, we can also take advantage of fruitful results developed in the Ô¨Āeld of contextual bandits (Russo and Van Roy, 2013; Foster et al., 2020). Decision making for regret minimization.To formulate the regret minimization problem, we consider the action setA= {+1,‚ąí1,‚ä•}, where the action+1 (resp. ‚ąí1) represents labeling any data pointx‚ąąX as positive (resp. negative); and the action‚ä•represents abstention. At each roundt‚ąą[T], the learner observes a data pointxt ‚ąąX (which can be chosen by an adaptive adversary), takes an actionat ‚ąąA, and then suÔ¨Äers a loss, which is deÔ¨Āned as ‚Ąďt(at) = 1 (sign(yt) Őł= at,at Őł= ‚ä•) + (1 2 ‚ąíő≥ ) ¬∑1 (at = ‚ä•). 10The version that works with an inÔ¨Ānite set of regression functions using concentration results presented in Lemma 4. The (conditionally) expected cumulative regret acrossT rounds is deÔ¨Āned as Regret(T) = T‚ąĎ t=1 Regrett, which is the target that the learner aims at minimizing. Selective querying for label eÔ¨Éciency. Besides choosing an actionat ‚ąąA at each time step, our algorithm also determineswhether or notto query the labelyt with respect toxt. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces the first computationally efficient active learning algorithm with abstention that achieves polylog(1/őĶ) label complexity without low-noise assumptions; establishes the proper abstention property (only abstains on near-boundary points), enabling translation to standard excess error and avoidance of noise-seeking behavior; provides minimax-optimal label complexity under Massart/Tsybakov noise for standard excess error via abstention-based learning; proves uncertainty-based active learners can fail under new noise-seeking conditions while the proposed method succeeds; extends to (i) constant label complexity using eluder dimension for finite function classes and (ii) model misspecification with guarantees when őļ ‚Č§ őĶ.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Formulates active learning with abstention using Chow‚Äôs loss (abstention cost 1/2 ‚ąí ő≥) and targets Chow‚Äôs excess error.
- Algorithm 1 (epoch-based): maintains an active set of regression functions via weighted square-loss regression oracle; constructs confidence intervals lcb/ucb for ő∑(x) from the active set; abstains if [lcb, ucb] ‚äÜ [1/2‚ąíő≥, 1/2+ő≥]; otherwise predicts sign(2 fŐām(x)‚ąí1); queries only when 1/2 lies inside (lcb, ucb) and the interval is not fully within the abstention band; label complexity analyzed via value-function disagreement coefficient őł.
- Efficient implementation: approximates lcb/ucb using regression-oracle-based subroutines; ensures monotone shrinking sampling region across epochs; oracle-call complexity given for general and convex F.
- Guarantees: Chow‚Äôs excess error ‚Č§ őĶ with label complexity O(őł¬∑Pdim(F)/ő≥^2 ¬∑ polylog(őł¬∑Pdim(F)/(őĶő≥))); proper abstention; conversion to standard error under Massart/Tsybakov with appropriate ő≥.
- Noise-seeking analysis: defines noise-seeking Massart/Tsybakov conditions allowing mass near ő∑=1/2; proves uncertainty-based learners can incur ő©(1/B) excess error while the proposed algorithm achieves őĶ with budget B ‚Č≥ (1/ő≥^2) polylog(1/(őĶő≥)).
- Algorithm 2 (constant label complexity): frames learning as regret minimization with selective querying; tighter elimination without epochs; outputs a random classifier from constructed sequence; label/query complexity analyzed via eluder dimension e; achieves label complexity O(e¬∑log(|F|/őī)/ő≥^2) independent of 1/őĶ.
- Algorithm 3 (misspecification): enlarges active sets to retain an approximating f within őļ of ő∑; achieves Chow‚Äôs excess error O(őĶ¬∑őł¬∑log(Pdim(F)/(őĶő≥őī))) and label complexity similar to Algorithm 1 when őļ ‚Č§ őĶ.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: No empirical experiments; validation is theoretical via proofs, concentration inequalities (Freedman), and complexity measures (pseudo-dimension, value-function disagreement coefficient, eluder dimension).
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Realizability assumed (ő∑ ‚ąą F) for main results; misspecification handled only when őļ ‚Č§ őĶ and yields weaker bounds (additive/multiplicative factors in őł and logs).
- Depends on access to efficient weighted square-loss regression oracles and on computing/approximating confidence bounds; practical scalability for complex F not empirically evaluated.
- Label complexity and oracle complexity depend on őł (value-function disagreement coefficient) and e (eluder dimension), which may be large or hard to compute/estimate for some classes.
- Results focused on binary classification and Chow‚Äôs abstention cost 1/2‚ąíő≥; extension to other losses/multiclass not provided.
- Minimax-optimal standard-error rates require choosing ő≥ using known noise parameters (Massart/Tsybakov), or budget-tuned ő≥; full adaptivity without such knowledge is not provided.
- No empirical benchmarks or comparisons; real-world performance and robustness untested.
- Constant label complexity requires finite F with bounded eluder dimension; general infinite classes covered only via őł-based bounds and may lose the constant property.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Remove or relax realizability and the őļ ‚Č§ őĶ requirement; develop algorithms with robust guarantees under general misspecification.
- Data-driven/adaptive selection of ő≥ and noise-parameter-free procedures achieving minimax rates.
- Extend to multiclass/multilabel settings and alternative abstention/coverage metrics (e.g., selective classification frameworks, risk‚Äďcoverage tradeoffs).
- Tighter bounds reducing dependence on őł, e, and ő≥; improved structural results on these complexity measures for richer hypothesis classes (e.g., deep networks).
- Practical implementations and large-scale empirical evaluations; efficient approximations to lcb/ucb in complex models.
- Batch-mode and pool-based variants; integration with modern representation learning and uncertainty quantification.
- Explore nonparametric regimes and characterize when abstention restores active-learning gains; sharper lower bounds linking computational and statistical trade-offs.
- Calibration and fairness considerations for abstention policies; human-in-the-loop workflows and cost-sensitive abstention designs.

new_method.method:
Motivation
- Problem focus („Éá„Éľ„āŅťĀłśäě„ĀģŚäĻÁéáśÄß): Modern subset selection breaks at web scale due to repeated retraining/re-scoring (SELCON/GLISTER), multi-seed averaging (EL2N/GraNd), or second-order solvers (influence/pruning). Operations teams need a single warm-up, single validation alignment, and a single, bounded-memory pass that yields nested subsets for any budget, compatible with continual refresh and privacy.
- Gap vs prior work:
  ‚ÄĘ Dynamics: Early-loss snapshots (EL2N/GraNd) ignore temporal geometry of SGD; influence/pruning uses costly H‚ąí1 approximations detached from streaming needs.
  ‚ÄĘ Objectives: Most methods optimize training proxies, not multi-objective validation targets (accuracy, fairness, calibration) in one pass.
  ‚ÄĘ Reusability: Few methods return budget-robust, nested subsets; AutoCoreset iterates over loss matrices; SELCON needs repeated Train calls.
- Why now: Foundation-model finetuning (LoRA/linear-head), streaming data, and hardware favor linear-time sketching and matrix‚Äďvector ops over iterative selection. A once-for-all, streaming, validation-aligned selector unlocks practical deployment at web scale with DP and continual updates.

Methodology
We propose One-Pass Sketch-and-Validate (OP-S&V): a streaming, validation-aligned, nested coreset builder based on temporal gradient sketches and an online dual controller. It is designed to be plug-in for PyTorch/JAX pipelines and FM finetuning.

Core pipeline (single warm-up, single validation pass, single streaming pass)
1) Temporal gradient sketching (short warm-up; bounded memory)
- Per-example head (or LoRA) gradients g_{i,t} from a brief warm-up. Optional deeper coverage via s randomized vJPs; compress each to dŐā with CountSketch/OSNAP and 8‚Äď16 bit quantization.
- Robust Frequent Directions (RFD) with exponential forgetting tracks the time-varying gradient subspace U ‚ąą R^{dŐā√ór}. Dynamic-rank scheduling increases r until spectral tail < őĶ_spec. Compute/memory: O(ndŐā) time, O(r¬∑dŐā) memory; runs fused on GPU.
- New vs draft: Temporal drift index. Maintain őĒ_spec = ||U_t U_t^T ‚ąí U_{t‚ąí1} U_{t‚ąí1}^T||_F as a cheap drift detector that gates when to refresh validation signals under distribution shift.

2) Single-shot validation-aligned control (one backprop per objective)
- Compute validation gradients v_q = ‚ąá_őł L_val,q(őł_T) for q in {accuracy, per-group fairness, differentiable calibration (temperature/ECE surrogate)}.
- Project and stabilize: vŐā_q = Proj_head ‚ąė Proj_U(P v_q) where P is light preconditioner (diag Fisher or block K-FAC built from warm-up batches); no H‚ąí1 solves.
- New vs prior: Multi-objective, constraint-first alignment. Specify target descent budgets ŌĄ_q and align selection probabilities so expected gradient contribution achieves ő£_i p_i‚ü®g_{i,T}, P vŐā_q‚ü© ‚Č§ ‚ąíŌĄ_q for all q, avoiding bilevel loops (SELCON/GLISTER).

3) One-pass scoring with online dual control
- Temporal novelty: r_i = ő£_t ||(I ‚ąí U U^T) g_{i,t}||¬≤ (late-t approximation allowed).
- Validation impact per objective: a_{i,q} = max(‚ąí‚ü®g_{i,T}, P vŐā_q‚ü©, 0)/(||P vŐā_q||+őĶ).
- Combined score: s_i(őĽ) = r_i + ő£_q őĽ_q a_{i,q} with a small sampling floor ő≥/n.
- Streaming őĽ updates: Maintain running estimates of E_p[‚ü®g, P vŐā_q‚ü©] while scanning once; update őĽ_q via projected subgradient with closed-form step sizes from Lipschitz bounds on a_{i,q}. Guarantees feasibility in one pass without retraining.
- New vs draft: Stability guard. If variance of running estimate exceeds a threshold, temporarily widen ő≥ to avoid over-concentrated sampling and resume once estimates stabilize.

4) Nested sampling and budget-free auto-stop
- Fixed-key weighted-reservoir sampling on s_i yields nestedness: larger budgets are supersets. Weights w_i = 1/(m p_i) with percentile clipping for optimizer stability.
- Auto-stop criteria: stop when (i) RFD spectral tail < őĶ_spec and (ii) projected validation residuals ||ő†_U P vŐā_q|| < őĶ_val for all q. This emits a once-for-all nested coreset without pre-specifying m.
- New vs prior: Mergeable coreset-of-coresets. Priority-union operator merges reservoirs from different shards/streams while preserving nestedness and unbiasedness.

5) Continual, fairness-aware, and DP-compliant
- Continual refresh: Keep RFD rolling with forgetting; recompute vŐā_q on schedule or drift trigger őĒ_spec > őļ. Only score new data; union reservoirs.
- Fairness/coverage: Add per-group probability floors or hinge penalties in s_i; include group-specific validation gradients v_q to prevent collapse.
- Differential privacy: Add Gaussian noise to sketch updates and to v_q before projection; release only U and {(i, w_i)}. Privacy accounting tight due to low-rank bounded releases.

6) Theory and diagnostics
- Sketch preservation: RFD guarantees preserve gradient energy up to őĶ_spec; first-order updates within span(U) stable.
- Validation descent: If weighted moment matching in projected space holds within őĺ, each objective decreases by at least ő∑‚ü®ő£_i w_i g_{i,T}, P vŐā_q‚ü© ‚ąí O(őĺ¬≤). Priority sampling concentration bounds control variance under floors.
- New: Drift-triggered regret. Bound cumulative validation regret between refreshes in terms of őĒ_spec and refresh interval, yielding an auto-refresh policy with sublinear regret.

7) Practical additions and code changes (PyTorch/JAX)
- New modules:
  ‚ÄĘ GradientSketcher: fused CountSketch + quantization + RFD with forgetting; exposes U and őĒ_spec.
  ‚ÄĘ ValAligner: computes v_q via a single autograd per objective; applies P and projections into U and head.
  ‚ÄĘ DualController: streaming őĽ updates with running moment trackers and stability guard; exposes p_i on-the-fly.
  ‚ÄĘ NestedReservoir: fixed-key weighted reservoir with priority-union and DP wrappers.
  ‚ÄĘ Diagnostics: spectral tail, moment residuals, drift index, őĽ trajectories, group coverage.
- Minimal hooks: register per-example head/LoRA gradients; optional s vJPs; one validation backward per objective.
- FM-friendly mode: restrict gradients to linear head or LoRA adapters; U then lives in a tiny parameter space while still steering selection for later full/partial finetuning.

How OP-S&V differs from prior studies
- vs SELCON: no iterative Train calls or MM loops; no submodular surrogates; streaming, single-pass őĽ control aligns directly to multi-objective validation without bilevel optimization; returns nested, once-for-all coresets and auto-stop.
- vs EL2N/GraNd: uses temporal gradient geometry instead of early loss-only proxies; eliminates multi-seed averaging; incorporates validation targets (accuracy, fairness, calibration) and preconditioning.
- vs AutoCoreset: avoids building an n√ót loss matrix; uses linear-time gradient sketches and priority sampling; guarantees nestedness and budget-free auto-stop.
- vs Influence/pruning: replaces H‚ąí1 solves with light preconditioning and low-rank projected alignment; no simulated annealing or expensive second order.
- vs Monotonic-kernel coresets: not restricted to monotone regularized losses; supports deep nets and non-monotone, multi-objective validation criteria.
- vs Dataset distillation: constructs real-data, weighted nested subsets; no meta-optimization or synthetic data.
- vs Prune4ReL and active learning: targets efficient training subset selection (not relabeling or label acquisition) and operates in one streaming pass with multi-objective validation control.

Expected impact
- Efficiency and simplicity: Order-of-magnitude reduction in selection time/memory via single warm-up + single validation pass + single streaming pass; bounded memory via sketches; one artifact reusable across any budget due to nestedness.
- Quality at small budgets: Temporal geometry preserves optimization-relevant directions; validation alignment steers accuracy, fairness, and calibration simultaneously; robust under continual refresh with drift triggers.
- Operational robustness: Budget-free auto-stop; diagnostics are actionable (spectral tail, residuals, drift); mergeable reservoirs support sharded/streaming data engineering workflows.
- Broad applicability: Deep/shallow classification and regression; foundation-model finetuning (head/LoRA); privacy-sensitive pipelines via DP OP-S&V; fairness-aware selection with explicit group controls.
- Deployment readiness: Minimal code intrusions (a few hooks/modules), hardware-friendly fused ops, and compatibility with existing PyTorch/JAX stacks enable adoption in production and large-scale research settings.
